Hi pdedmon,

Thank you for your pull request!

The Travis failure is due to some lines exceeding 80 characters. Not a huge deal. I have a pre-commit hook I use to warn me about these things but I am not sure what is the best way to share it in the repository.

My initial thoughts with regards to the issues tagged `user customization` were to allow them to be fixed by refactoring the code into a `Decoder` and `Encoder` model similar to that used by the python standard library for JSON.

I will take a closer look at this PR this week to see how I feel about your proposed solution. It may be easier to fix #100 and #77 together since they both will rely on the unmangled `toml` source.

Sorry for the long silence. Every time I have to use `pip` I still dream about getting back to my rewrite, then quickly get lost / scared at how deep the Python packaging rabbit hole goes.

My `pi` package did add a bit of (admittedly hack-ish) functionality to setuptools, and there might be some system out there that uses it for some legacy reason, and depends on getting it from PyPI. I highly doubt it, but it's a possibility, and in general it's bad practice to replace, wholesale, a project on a public / permanent artifact repository (like PyPI) with one that is totally different.

So I'd like to hang onto the name for now, though if your project becomes immensely popular in the future and has good reason to need the `pi` name, I would reconsider.

Merged.

Thank you, again, Pedro. Don't hesitate to let me know if there's anything I can do for you.

I just ran into this too. I think it's just the import of ipdb which prevents pickle from pickling any functions defined in __main__. See this IPython bug (still open unfortunately) for more details:
https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/1252

When I proposed this, I had in mind **simplicity**, no params. Why needing to remember some not-obvious parameters when we can have a simple "alias" that does not need explanations?

If we would have something like `@retry(count=2)` probably this would not be needed, but current syntax is not very friendly and considerably more verbose. I think I know why we have this but this should not providing a simple and clean syntax for the most used use-cases.

Note: I am currently using `flaky` for retrying unit tests and the default behaviour is to try twice instead of infinitely, something that I like. I am aware that changing defaults would be out of the question as nobody likes broken APIs.

The only solution that I see is to iterate before ever trying the first call. That sounds like something dangerous. Best thing is probably to document that the caller is responsible for not passing generators.

Calling [Flower API's](http://flower.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#post--api-task-apply-(.+)) `/api/task/apply/*` is just hanging without the results returning, even though the task was processed and succeeded according to the Web UI.

Reproducing the error:
```
    # hangs even though task succeeded in logs
    curl -d '{"args": [1, 2]}' -H "Content-Type: application/json" -X POST http://0.0.0.0:5000/api/task/apply/tasks.add
```
Checking the task by id just shows it as pending even though the Flower UI shows it as a success and I also see the task result in the worker logs.
```
    curl -X GET http://0.0.0.0:5000/api/task/result/02fa2656-c92e-44e6-a2af-c04e666e3f89

I doubt you'll ever encounter a TLS Handshake packet in the wild that 
has no extensions. It is not permitted by protocol, and it would be 
impossible to establish a TLS connection without the use of extensions 
in the Handshake packet.

If you'd like I can add code to test that there are extensions before 
parsing them. If that would resolve this.

It is possible for an extension to have data of zero length. But

> returned as list of tuples, such as [(ext1_type, ext1_value),

Section 7.4.1.4 of RFC 5246 says:
When multiple extensions of different types are present in the
ClientHello or ServerHello messages, the extensions MAY appear in any
order.  There MUST NOT be more than one extension of the same type.

I checked the RFCs that update RFC 5246 and this text is still normative.

Given the above I think using a Python dict makes sense.

Correct.

> ist it possible to detect non canonical splice site w/ this option?

Minimap2 doesn't require canonical sites; it just prefers it. I would recommend to reduce option `-C` instead. With something like `-C5` or even smaller, you will see more non-canonical splice sites.

You are probably aligning against a "multi-part" index. In this case, minimap2 is unable to output a proper header. You have to manually add it (e.g. with samtools view -b -T ref.fa in.sam > out.bam) as a post step.

Ok, this is as good as I want to make it right now. Unfortunately after getting all the work in to enable use with SQLAlchemy+sqlite it turns out the BSD backend is 3 times faster than the SQLAlchemy+sqlite backend during loading.

Even worse, cache population using SQLAlchemy+sqlite segfaults on a trimmed-down PG catalog with 2024 entries in it (BSD backend completes in 153 seconds on the same dataset). It's certainly possible that pointing SQLAlchemy towards a real DB might be faster and succeed on larger data sets, but that seems very heavy-handed for this use-case.

I think the metadata cache manager is still immensely useful for more fine-grained control over the backend cache, particularly when being used by a web service, but the use of SQLAlchemy+sqlite is not a silver bullet for making it faster. I suspect for that we'd have to move off of an RDF backend altogether and onto a backend (possibly sqlite) with a custom schema.

```

with open(OUTPUT_FILE, 'rb') as d:
    output = PdfReader(d)

for i, page in enumerate(output.pages):

    # Do this to get original page dimensions
    pp = PageMerge().add(watermark).add(page)

    wm = pp[0]
    p = pp[1]

    wm.scale(A4[0] / wm.w, A4[1] / wm.h)
    p.scale(A4[0] / p.w, A4[1] / p.h)

    pr = pp.render()

    # These need to be added manually
    # So we replace data on the existing page
    page.Resources = pr.Resources
    page.Contents = pr.Contents
    page.MediaBox = pr.MediaBox
    page.CropBox = pr.CropBox

    # Another way to add the watermark, but
    # this way we can not resize the contents.
    # Add watermark to ORIGINAL page.

```python
#redis handle
from pyspider.libs.base_handler import *
import redis
import json

class RedisHandle(BaseHandler):
    def __init__(self):
        self.redis = redis.StrictRedis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0)
        
    def on_result(self, result):
        data = json.dumps(result ,ensure_ascii=False)
        self.redis.lpush('pyspider_list',  data)
        super(RedisHandle, self).on_result(result)
```

Original comment by: Jonathan Ballet

Recent refactoring of the CryptedFileKeyring means that keyring file creation (and this directory creation) now depends on the same file creation as found in BasicFileKeyring (as all operations now defer to set_password rather than creating files directly). Therefore, this issue should be resolved in 0.9.2. Please see #59 for a pre-release sdist (and notes of caution) you may test against.

Original comment by: Jason R. Coombs

They aren't accessible by any script. They're only accessed by a script run in the user's authenticated context, which is already authorized by the user's login.

You're somewhat right that it's less secure than requiring a password on each entry, which is less secure than not using keyring at all and requiring each password to be entered uniquely.

If security is paramount, then using keyring may not be the best option - or using a custom keyring provider that requires a password entry on each invocation.

However, there are many use cases where the current implementation provides a suitable balance of convenience and security. Changing that expectation without consideration for those cases would trouble for those users.

The current implementation is also well-aligned with the Windows keyring implementation, which similarly relies on the user's authenticated context to grant access to the keyring. In Windows, multiple applications can access the keyring, such that a password for a given resource can be shared by different applications in different contexts. I use this feature in Windows to access passwords from Powershell, without Python or keyring being involved at all.

So it would be inappropriate to make this change via fiat. A change that drastically impacts the API like this should be introduced incrementally, as a separate keyring backend, and as that backend gains acceptance, it might be considered to be the default backend, especially if these referenced vulnerabilities were shown to be impactful.

from jwt.algorithms import ECAlgorithm
from jwt.contrib.algorithms.py_ecdsa import ECAlgorithm as LegacyAlgorithm

# Sign a message with the legacy algorithm
legacy = LegacyAlgorithm(LegacyAlgorithm.SHA256)
legacy_key = legacy.prepare_key(ec_key)

The Pi2 peripherals are exactly the same as the earlier Pis.  See the bcm3835 documentation.  The peripheral base address has moved from 0x20000000 to 0x3F000000.  DMA memory is now at offset 0xC0000000 rather than 0x40000000.  For RPIO.GPIO you should be allocating DMA memory via the mailbox interface.

TLDR: you should check for None in the returns from fit_seasons or adjust_seasons, it means no seasonal component was detected.

You're fitting a seasonal + trend model to data that is generated by a different process (you're summing a low-amplitude sine wave and a parabola). For short sequences, a linear trend is a decent fit to your quadratic term, "decent" meaning that after subtracting it from the data we can resolve the underlying sine wave as a seasonal component. But longer sequences diverge enough from linear that their residuals swamp the amplitude of your sine wave and it is no longer detected.

This functionality basically mimics `pydash.at`:

```python
>>> pydash.at({'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}, 'a', 'b')
[1, 2]
```

So we could add a new `properties()` function:

```python
# pydash/utilities.py

def properties(*paths):
    return lambda obj: [getter(obj)
                        for getter in (pyd.property_(path) for path in paths)]
```

Then `iteratee()` could use `properties()` when a tuple is passed in (like in this PR) to support deep property mapping:

```python
>>> iteratee(('a', 'b', ['c', 'd', 'e']))({'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': {'d': {'e': 3}}})
[1, 2, 3]
```

Later, I can modify `at()` to use `properties()` under the hood for deep property support there too.

Also; don't forget that there is a turn-around time, awaiting the response from the client device.

I've rewritten your example to be a bit more correct:

```
import socket
import time
from cpppo.server.enip import client
plc_ip_address          = "127.0.0.1"
timeout                 = 5.0

while True:
    try:
        with client.connector( host= plc_ip_address, timeout=timeout ) as conn:
            while True:
                data = [int(0) for c in range( 5 )]
                req = conn.write( "CDS_PLC_RawData_TLC", elements=len( data ), data=data,
                              tag_type=client.enip.DINT.tag_type )
                rpy,ela = client.await( conn, timeout=timeout )
                print rpy

    except AssertionError:
        print "Response timed out!! Tearing Connection and Reconnecting!!!!!"
    except AttributeError:
        print "Tag J1_pos not written:::Will try again::"
    except socket.error as exc:
        print "Couldn't send command: %s" % ( exc )

    time.sleep( .1 )
```

All this is sort of taken care of, if you use the client.connector API's simple 'process' method.  This performs all tag I/O (allows pipeline depth, multiple request packets, etc., if desired), and synchronously returns the list of response after all I/O is complete:

```
#
# Run simulator in another window:
#     python -m cpppo.server.enip -va localhost --print CDS_PLC_RawData_TLC=DINT[4]
#

import socket
import time
import cpppo
from cpppo.server.enip import client
plc_ip_address                  = "127.0.0.1"
timeout                         = 5.0

import logging
#cpppo.log_cfg['level'] = logging.DETAIL
logging.basicConfig( **cpppo.log_cfg )

tags                            = ["CDS_PLC_RawData_TLC[0-3]=(DINT)0,1,2,3"]
while True:
    try:

        with client.connector( host=plc_ip_address, timeout=timeout ) as conn:
            operations          = client.parse_operations( tags )
            failures,replies    = conn.process(
                operations=operations, timeout=timeout )
        for rpy in replies:
            print rpy

    except Exception as exc:
        print "EtherNet/IP I/O Failed: %s" % ( exc )
    time.sleep( .1 )
```

Two things to try.

1. cPickle - uses the same interface, but is much faster.
```python
import cPickle as pickle
pickle.dump(cl,f)
```
2. Use a newer version of the Pickle protocol. The default is backwards compatible, but also old and slow compared with newer implementations.  See this answer on [StackOverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/a/23582505/4108524).
```python
pickle.dump(cl, f, -1)  # -1 forces highest protocol
```

In my test using 2000 records, these two changes reduced the time to create `textclassifier.pickle` from `32 seconds` to `12`, the load time from `11 seconds` to `5`, and the file size from `354M` to `245M`.

It is a syntax + semantics issue. A syntax `routes[key]` always returns one route, even if there are many with the same key. And I don't dare to change this behaviour — people use it and the change will break the existing API.

Now about possible solutions. The `routes` object (or `routes.tables[x]`) is _not_ a dictionary. It is a list-like object. The notation `[key]` is handled by the method to return the first match. So one obvious solution:
```
>>> from pyroute2 import IPDB
>>> ip = IPDB()
# returns the first match
>>> ip.routes['fe80::/64']
{'metrics': {}, 'oif': 3, 'dst_len': 64, 'family': 10, 'proto': 2, 'tos': 0, 'dst': 'fe80::/64', 'pref': '00', 'ipdb_priority': 0, 'priority': 256, 'flags': 0, 'src_len': 0, 'table': 254, 'multipath': [], 'type': 1, 'scope': 0, 'ipdb_scope': 'system'}
# returns all the matches
>>> [x for x in ip.routes if x['dst'] == 'fe80::/64']
[{'metrics': {}, 'oif': 3, 'dst_len': 64, 'family': 10, 'proto': 2, 'tos': 0, 'dst': 'fe80::/64', 'pref': '00', 'ipdb_priority': 0, 'priority': 256, 'flags': 0, 'src_len': 0, 'table': 254, 'multipath': [], 'type': 1, 'scope': 0, 'ipdb_scope': 'system'}, {'metrics': {}, 'oif': 6, 'dst_len': 64, 'family': 10, 'proto': 2, 'tos': 0, 'dst': 'fe80::/64', 'pref': '00', 'ipdb_priority': 0, 'priority': 256, 'flags': 0, 'src_len': 0, 'table': 254, 'multipath': [], 'type': 1, 'scope': 0, 'ipdb_scope': 'system'}]
```

Another solution we can introduce. It can look like that: `routes["default", "all"]` would return all matches, not only the first one.

@foodpoison the toc and the navMap are effectively the same thing, just one from epub 2 and the other from epub 3. From [the epub content documents 3.0 documentation](http://www.idpf.org/epub/30/spec/epub30-contentdocs.html#sec-xhtml-nav-def-types-toc):

> The `toc` `nav` element corresponds to the `navMap` element in the superseded NCX

Just as the navMap was required in Epub 2, the toc is required in Epub 3.  One of the IDPF sample epubs is for [a trivial, one-page epub](https://github.com/IDPF/epub3-samples/releases/download/20170606/childrens-media-query.epub), and you can see [its navMap](https://github.com/IDPF/epub3-samples/blob/master/30/childrens-media-query/EPUB/toc.ncx#L14-L21) and [its toc](https://github.com/IDPF/epub3-samples/blob/master/30/childrens-media-query/EPUB/toc.xhtml).

In order to be a legal epub it needs at least a minimal toc, even if that minimal toc contains only one entry pointing to the main content. My suspicion is that this is why @aerkalov's code requires that you create one in order to get the other.

Hi @tkossak,

`SMBFS` relies on the NetBIOS naming service to query the name from the IP, or the IP from the name. It could be that the NetBIOS service is not running on your server, or that it's running in a non-standard port (which is supposed to be `137`)

You could check if its with the following snippet:
```python
import nmb.NetBIOS
nb = nmb.NetBIOS.NetBIOS()
response = nb.queryIPForName("<IP>")  # with you IP here obviously

IIRC, the `ids` are used by the Java code that is old consumer, before the self-balancing consumer. pykafka copies this Java style of sticking an "ids" and "owners" subnodes in zookeeper, which is why you see that. 

But kafka-python's new KafkaConsumer is a new style / self-balancing consumer, and actually shouldn't create anything under zookeeper's `/consumers` for this consumer group. 

Assuming you're only using kafka-python for this consumer group, then you should remove the subnode for it from zookeeper `/consumers`. If it comes back, then somewhere something other than kafka-python is creating a consumer group with that same name... you'd need to track that down and kill it.

This is definitely a broker configuration issue, unrelated to kafka-python. The topic that is failing to create is `__consumer_offsets` . Try setting `offsets.topic.replication.factor` to 1 on your single broker.

I was trying to track down a way to reliable find out what unit `/proc/meminfo` reports in (my display shows `kB`).  I came across [this in the RHEL docs](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-US/Red_Hat_Enterprise_Linux/6/html/Deployment_Guide/s2-proc-meminfo.html).

> While the file shows kilobytes (kB; 1 kB equals 1000 B), it is actually kibibytes (KiB; 1 KiB equals 1024 B). This imprecision in /proc/meminfo is known, but is not corrected due to legacy concerns - programs rely on /proc/meminfo to specify size with the "kB" string.

Also [this post in the Ubuntu forums](https://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1165076).

>I'm sure that it must be kiB, here's why:
>
> free, references /proc/meminfo, If I cat /proc/meminfo it gives me:
> MemTotal: 2797796 kB
> 
> Now, if I run free -b, I get 2864943104 bytes total.
> 
> 2864943104 / 1024 = 2797796
> 
> That is the same amount that is reported by /proc/meminfo

```
if [[ "$COMPREPLY" =~ [/=:]$ ]]; then
    compopt -o nospace
fi
```

**Gathering implementation thoughts:**

This is going to be kind of complex and might take a couple prototypes to get right. 

Probably ties with https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/75 to some extent. 

Simplest approach: 

* Each canonical test gets a clone with dynamic fields substituted, just like [calling the current realize method[(https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/pyresttest/tests.py#L241)
  - Easy to implement I think (create properties for each item, with getters that use an internally bound context at runtime to generate result, then do a lookup for each field this way) 
  - Ex: when parsing with a body from generator, a property is created instead of setting the value.  Property does string.template(_body).safe_substitute(context.get_variables()) or similar. 
  - Bad for benchmarks because they would require creating a copy every time.  But for these if we move the execution into the benchmark class itself, it can do some internal optimization here!
* Full-dynamic property access, using bound context:
  - Canonical test/benchmark steps are copied for runtime use and reused - the context is bound in, and properties are used similar to above, however there is no full 'realize' step applied ahead of time.  Each property access dynamically evaluates, and benchmark may be called with the same context multiple times.

Harder approach:
* Try to anticipate the sequence of values, I.E. pre-generate generator results?

The philosophy of the pandoc-fignos filter (and friends) is to allow cross-referencing with as little fuss as possible for pdf/TeX and html, and other formats where possible.  The cleveref fakery was introduced to mitigate users having to manually insert the cleveref package into their latex template.  If you want to use the cleveref package than you can turn off cleveref fakery by setting `xnos-cleveref-fake` to `False`.

For a source - lens system, we can perform all lens modeling without knowledge of the lens / source redshifts and other quantities. However, we use those quantities to translate coordinates from arcseconds to physical distances (parsecs) as well as compute physical quantities for things like mass. However, that's something we'd typically do after the actual lens modeling and analysis. 

The 'mass_within_circles' routine is an example of one currently in Galaxy, where I'm using the critical density to convert the dimensionless mass to a physical mass. I guess we could move stuff like this to the RayTracingPlane class, e.g. 'mass_within_circle_of_lens_galaxies'. This makes me think having LensGalaxy, SourceGalaxy and LensAndSourceGalaxy classes could be worthwhile, as this dictates what quantities you can or can't measure for galaxy. Lets not do that yet though!

My immediate plan is to get the ray tracing calculation set up and working for a lens-source system, to give us a feel for how it all shapes up and looks. It gets pretty confusing when you're interfacing it with the image data, light/mass profiles and source-planes, so I think this'll help us make progress. 

However, as said above, if we have >2 galaxies, the ray-tracing code is necessary for deflection angle calculations and therefore lens modeling. So well need to figure all this out sooner or later, but I suspect once we have the 2 system case sorted itll sorta design itself.

Hi @sobolevn!
> What is the way to configure vulture? As I get it now the only possible way is command line arguments. Which can be rather long or complex. A common way to configure something in python is supporting both .vulture.ini and setup.cfg. I personally prefer setup.cfg.

Yes, indeed, command line options are the only way to configure vulture right now. Having a `.vulture.ini` file would be an overkill IMHO for such a simple task. You can always use aliases if you find the commands are too long.

> Is it possible to ignore some lines from audit? For example, I use better-exceptions sometimes. It only requires to write import better_exceptions. I can ignore this line from pylint/flake8 by using import better_exceptions # noqa instead. As I can right now it is not supported.

To avoid it being detected as dead code, you can whitelist `better_exceptions`, just create an additional file named `whitelist.py` (or whatever you wish to name it - it doesn't matter):
```py
from vulture.whitelist_utils import Whitelist

whitelist = Whitelist()
whitelist.better_exceptions
```

Just make sure to include this file when you run vulture, for ex.
```
vulture your_file.py whitelist.py
```

You won't need to explicitly mention this if `whitelist.py` appears in your root folder.
Also, keep in mind that we only have to hit the red lines, not the orange ones (which are already executed by a subset of the tested interpreters).

I don't understand how your example script could possibly have worked for you. You're using `BackgroundScheduler` there, which of course does not block the script from exiting so it exits right after you start it. When I replaced it with `BlockingScheduler` and moved the `scheduler.start()` line after the `scheduler.add_job()` line, it all works as expected. Please try with the attached script.
```
import logging

from apscheduler.schedulers.blocking import BlockingScheduler
from apscheduler.jobstores.sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemyJobStore
from apscheduler.executors.pool import ThreadPoolExecutor

sqlalchemy_job_store = SQLAlchemyJobStore(url='sqlite:////tmp/ap.db', tablename='scheduler')
job_stores = {'default': sqlalchemy_job_store}
executors = {'default': ThreadPoolExecutor(20)}
scheduler = BlockingScheduler(job_stores=job_stores, executors=executors)


def add(x, y):
    print("adding...")
    return x + y


logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)
scheduler.add_job(add, 'interval', args=(6, 6), seconds=30)
scheduler.start()
```

I don't think this is the correct fix for the problem at hand. Trying to encode only once when asked to encode multiple times simply sounds wrong, and besides, I don't think it can be made to work properly with the current implementation. It has already in its current git master form caused a regression, for example this on a 1.2 connection:
```python
 conn.send("/queue/test", "test", headers={"test": r'\c\n'})
```
...ends up sending ```test:\c\n``` on the wire which is incorrect; both backslashes should be doubled.

Apart from modifying the passed in headers, I think the previous code worked just fine. In any case, I suggest reverting the changes that attempt to avoid encoding when already encoded, and instead either:
1) Simply documenting the fact that ```send_frame()``` modifies the passed in headers, or
2) Making either ```send_frame()``` or ```utils.Frame()``` copy the headers and operate on the copy

By the way, all internal callers of ```send_frame()``` in stomp.py seem to pass it a copy of the headers they receive, copied through ```utils.merge_headers()```. My vote would go to alternative 1) above in order to avoid some header copies in the vast majority of cases that do not need it. ```send_frame()``` is not documented to be an API method by the way, so maybe it should be explicitly made private while at it (both in docs and by adding an underscore prefix).

`Session.__exit__` calls `ThreadPoolExecutor.shutdown`. "cancel" is a slightly strong word for what's happening, but still accurate. In-progress requests won't be immediately killed/abandoned, they will complete as normal. Anything still queued and not yet started won't be kicked off though as the executor won't pick them up. Killing the in-flight stuff could be messy and potentially cause problems in cases where the script will continue to live on and do other work.

If you specifically need to kill things immediately you might take a look at [canceling the exector's _threads](https://gist.github.com/clchiou/f2608cbe54403edb0b13). I haven't tried that so no clue how well it'd work and what sort of state that would leave things in. 

If you want to PR something that elaborates on the cancel behavior I'll take a look at it.

Hi,

there is a recent trend to report tokenized BLEU (with multi-bleu.perl) 
on newstest2014. For the sake of comparison, I report such numbers in 
this paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/1612.04629

It's a small pet-peeve of mine that reporting tokenized BLEU scores is a 
bad practice in the field, because scores will be affected by your 
choice of tokenization scripts and settings. I typically report BLEU 
with the NIST BLEU script (mteval-v13a.perl), which takes untokenized 
text and performs its own internal tokenization, which guarantees 
consistency across systems. I've also created a variant of 
multi-bleu.perl that performs the same internal tokenization (expecting 
untokenized references and hypotheses), and produces the same scores, as 
mteval-v13a.perl: 
https://github.com/rsennrich/nematus/blob/master/data/multi-bleu-detok.perl

best wishes,
Rico

```python
import urllib, xml.dom.minidom
def Geocoding_International_Geocode_v1_10(Key, Country, Location):

      #Build the url
      requestUrl = "http://services.postcodeanywhere.co.uk/Geocoding/International/Geocode/v1.10/xmla.ws?"
      requestUrl += "&" +  urllib.urlencode({"Key":Key})
      requestUrl += "&" +  urllib.urlencode({"Country":Country})
      requestUrl += "&" +  urllib.urlencode({"Location":Location})

      #Get the data
      dataDoc = xml.dom.minidom.parseString(urllib.urlopen(requestUrl).read())

      #Get references to the schema and data
      schemaNodes = dataDoc.getElementsByTagName("Column")
      dataNotes = dataDoc.getElementsByTagName("Row")

      #Check for an error
      if len(schemaNodes) == 4 and schemaNodes[0].attributes["Name"].value == "Error":
         raise Exception, dataNotes[0].attributes["Description"].value

      #Work though the items in the response
      results = []
      for dataNode in dataNotes:
         rowData = dict()
         for schemaNode in schemaNodes:
              key = schemaNode.attributes["Name"].value
              value = dataNode.attributes[key].value
              rowData[key] = value
         results.append(rowData)

       return results

      #FYI: The output is an array of key value pairs, the keys being:
      #Name
      #Latitude
      #Longitude
 ```

This seems like it could go very badly wrong with examples like this:

```python
@dataclass
class ObjectRelationalMappedRow:
    database_connection: DatabaseConnection = global_database_connection
```

i.e. there are many cases where a shared, mutable default is what is intended, and `copy.copy` will produce deeply surprising behaviors, especially given the fact that its behavior is *opt out* and not opt in, so objects like this hypothetical `DatabaseConnection` now need to go to some additional trouble to be non-copyable if they don't happen to *directly* contain a non-copyable object like a socket (since this isn't deepcopy, if the socket is one attribute "hop" away, you won't notice the silent implied state corruption).

No, your definition is perfectly fine. `WS` from your grammar is used in entirely different way. It has nothing to do with `ws` Parser param. You can call your rules in `LAYOUT` part of grammar anyway you like, only the entry rule must be called `LAYOUT`. Matching using grammar rules is done [here](https://github.com/igordejanovic/parglare/blob/master/parglare/parser.py#L541) using regex and string matches from terminal definitions. If the match succeeds the position is incremented by the length of the match, so your `WS` will greadilly consume all whitespaces ahead in one go.

You can do that by running a modify that ignores its argument and just takes values from the iterator:

    format = lens.Each()[1]['index'].modify(lambda _: next(index_generator))

Note that you will need to make sure that your iterator is large enough or else you will get really weird errors (currently, your range has 7 items, but your lens has 8 foci). Also, dictionaries are not guaranteed to iterate in any particular order prior to python 3.6, so the keys and the indices may not match up.

I agree with what you say in point 1.

Regarding point 2, a little extra knowledge off the top of my head might help. Each channel (including the connection, which is defined by AMQP to be channel 0) is essentially an _actor_ with an inbox of frames from the server. The channel autonomously pulls frames out of the inbox and processes them using the correct method in the [`Handler`](https://github.com/benjamin-hodgson/asynqp/blob/2b3f32c13d62c5bfe9e579d8835a10cefd3c4dfa/src/asynqp/channel.py#L185). The `Handler` is basically the entry point for a channel to process a frame.

The inbox is implemented as a `Queue` - there is one queue per open channel; these are managed by the [`Dispatcher`](https://github.com/benjamin-hodgson/asynqp/blob/f59a6ec158ac068c9c49cc1b9422266eaad03b15/src/asynqp/routing.py#L10). So when a frame arrives, [the `Protocol` tells the `Dispatcher`](https://github.com/benjamin-hodgson/asynqp/blob/f59a6ec158ac068c9c49cc1b9422266eaad03b15/src/asynqp/protocol.py#L32) to put the frame in the queue of the correct channel.

The `Synchroniser` is basically an implementation detail of the handler; it keeps track of which objects on a channel are waiting for which frames and notifies them when a frame arrives. It mediates between API classes such as `Queue` and the objects which respond to the server (the `Handler`s).

So given all that, I think that error handling should respect the current architecture and go via the `Dispatcher` and the `Handler`. I think the right way to go is to add a special type of message which kills the actor (this is usually called a "poison pill") and a corresponding method on the `Handler` to respond to it. I guess this method would tell its `Synchroniser` to kill all of its currently-live `Futures`.

That sounds like quite a lot of work to me. If you want to submit a pull request for part 1 (raising exceptions from the `Protocol` when the connection dies) and leave part 2 (the poison pill message) to me then I'm fine with that. But if you fancy a challenge then I'll be very happy to accept a contribution for each of them!

I had a few observations about the tests for the future:
1. You could make the test server port dynamic (in the ephemeral range) by binding to `0` and then retrieving it with `socket.getsockname()` which nicely avoids any potential for conflict.
2. I notice that a lot of the validation is done through the `history` system. It would be a more "black box" test to verify the client using the raw data that the server receives.
3. To reduce the likelihood of non-compliance in the server masking non-compliance in the client, you could avoid running an implementation of jsonrpc on the server and instead just use some pretty static response logic. This would then also allow you to easily have less "happy-path" tests where the server intentionally responds abnormally. The drawback with this route is you'd probably then want separate tests for your server implementation.

If you want, I can apply the above ideas in the tests that I'll be adding for the _Method changes unless you'd prefer consistency with the other tests. Let me know.

from exchangelib import DELEGATE, IMPERSONATION, Account, Credentials, \
    EWSDateTime, EWSTimeZone, Configuration, NTLM, CalendarItem, Message, \
    Mailbox, Attendee, Q
from exchangelib.folders import Calendar, ExtendedProperty, FileAttachment, ItemAttachment, \
    HTMLBody

     server='email.domain.com',

)

                   access_type=DELEGATE)

tz = EWSTimeZone.timezone('Australia/Brisbane')
item = CalendarItem(folder=account.calendar, subject='foo')
 
# Build a list of calendar items
calendar_items = []
for hour in range(7, 17):
    calendar_items.append(CalendarItem(
        start=tz.localize(EWSDateTime(year, month, day, hour, 30)),
        end=tz.localize(EWSDateTime(year, month, day, hour + 1, 15)),
        subject='Test item',
        body='Hello from Python',
        location='devnull',
        categories=['foo', 'bar'],
        required_attendees = [Attendee(
            mailbox=Mailbox(email_address='user1@example.com'),
            response_type='Accept'
        )]
    ))
 
# bulk_update() and bulk_delete() methods are also supported.

Just ran into this issue today. My solution was to provide a custom render_item function to the context.

```
def render_item(type_, obj, autogen_context):
    """Apply custom rendering for selected items."""

    if type_ == 'type' and isinstance(obj, sqlalchemy_utils.types.uuid.UUIDType):
        # add import for this type
        autogen_context.imports.add("import sqlalchemy_utils")
        autogen_context.imports.add("import uuid")
        return "sqlalchemy_utils.types.uuid.UUIDType(), default=uuid.uuid4"

    # default rendering for other objects
    return False


def run_migrations_online():
    # ---snip---
    with connectable.connect() as connection:
        context.configure(
            connection=connection,
            target_metadata=target_metadata,
            render_item=render_item
        )

        with context.begin_transaction():
            context.run_migrations()
```

As I don't use that code myself, I'm waiting for someone who does to supply the appropriate tests and fixes, as described in the project status section in the README - https://github.com/spookylukey/django-paypal#project-status

Yes, we don't support this, and there wouldn't be an easy way to build in support for all the possibilities.

There are a few approaches you could take:

1) Just copy and paste the generated HTML

2) If this is not sufficient e.g. you need the notify_url and other parameters, then merge together the two forms - the one generated by django-paypal and the one from the PayPal Sandbox site.

For both these cases, you won't be using django-paypal to finally generate the form, you will just have a block of HTML in your template. This has the disadvantage that things like the form action URL won't adjust automatically for test mode.

3) Do not attempt to pass into paypal_dict the fields that need to be a drop down box. Instead of using `{{ form.render }}`, use `{{ form.as_p }}`. This will render out only the hidden fields. To this, you will then need to manually add the `<form>` tag, and the other bits you need - the `<select>` drop down for example.

4) Subclass `PayPalPaymentsForm` and add a field for the dropdown, using the normal Django forms API - https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/topics/forms/ . In this way you can add a visible field to the form for the `<select>` (all the predefined fields on this form are hidden). This is the neatest solution, but perhaps requires you to understand more. I've used it successfully in the past - e.g. see https://bitbucket.org/spookylukey/cciw-website/src/7c777391cac2320b798997a652372edad3b5fe6a/cciw/bookings/views.py?at=default&fileviewer=file-view-default#views.py-803 where I have a form that allows people to put in the amount that they want to pay in some cases. In your cases you would be using a `choices` rather than letting people enter what they wanted to, and probably for a different field. But the principle is the same.

Also, as long as you and your colleagues agree, what I think is irrelevant. And if one day the Ansible community agrees on a single standard, we'll likely modify our internal standard.

If you are going to implement this, my thoughts are that it should be in config.ini - something like

```
[ansible-review]
indent_before_hyphen = true
indent_spaces = 4
```

Not sure I have a better way to do it, but welcome thoughts.

From an Ansible point of view, spaces should be irrelevant as long as the yaml parser works. And it'll work with no, two or four or more spaces before a hyphen.

Did some digging. The saving breaks if there is multilevel nesting and the `extra` attribute in `NestedStackedInline` is set to `0`. It means that the sub-sub-level forms do not get created. Attached some code to reproduce:

```
# models.py
from django.db import models


class A(models.Model):
	content = models.CharField(max_length=255)


class B(models.Model):
	content = models.CharField(max_length=255)
	a = models.ForeignKey(A)


class C(models.Model):
	content = models.CharField(max_length=255)
	b = models.ForeignKey(B)
```

```
#admin.py

from django.contrib import admin
from nested_inline.admin import NestedStackedInline, NestedModelAdmin
from someapp.models import A, B, C


class CAdmin(NestedStackedInline):
	model = C
	extra = 1


class BAdmin(NestedStackedInline):
	model = B
	inlines = [CAdmin]
	extra = 0 #  <-- This breaks the formset generation


class AAdmin(NestedModelAdmin):
	inlines = [BAdmin]
	model = A

admin.site.register(A, AAdmin)
```

I don't think the syntax `log.info('The value is: %s', some_value)` is incorrect, because it is described in the Python 2.7 logging documentation and howtos. This is relevant (I think not only) for me, because I use libraries like SQLAlchemy, etc. that use that syntax too.

For me a workaround using a filter that creates the message mimics the behaviour:
```
class CMRESFixFilter(logging.Filter):
    def filter(self, record):
        if not hasattr(record, 'message'):
            record.message = record.getMessage()
        return 1
```
and adding `'args'` to `__LOGGING_FILTER_FIELDS` as the serialization might fail (_TypeError: Unable to serialize xyz_) when the log record is sent.

1. The easiest way to tell if it's using the GPUs is to look at your Spark executor logs, you should see something like this:
```
2018-03-20 20:16:35,228 INFO (MainThread-26716) 1: ======== worker:0 ========
2018-03-20 20:16:35,228 INFO (MainThread-26716) 1: Cluster spec: {'ps': ['10.213.54.198:46866'], 'worker': ['10.213.54.198:39733', '10.213.54.206:43606', '10.213.54.198:33758']}
2018-03-20 20:16:35,334 INFO (MainThread-26716) 1: Using GPU: 6
```

2. If you've installed all the dependencies on your nodes, then you shouldn't need to set most of these variables.

3/4. These are setting the `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` env var on the executors, which is used by the various bits of code which require these native libs.  If you've set `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` accordingly on all of your cluster nodes, then you will not need to pass them thru the Spark conf.

5. pyspark applications can only be started via spark-submit.  However, you can use the pyspark shell for interactive sessions, which might work fine for you (if you've supplied all the dependencies on all nodes), so something like `pyspark --master yarn` might be enough to get you going.

@TaoLv provides very informative questions.

FYI, two points I'd like to mention:

- **MKL-DNN profile information**
When the new CPU backend is enabled from 1.0.1, most of the execution time will be inside MKL-DNN library such as convolution, batch normalization. And we can get the execution time of MKL-DNN by setting MKLDNN_VERBOSE=1, [details](https://github.com/01org/mkl-dnn/blob/master/doc/perf_profile.md). 
The output includes lots of very useful information for the performance debug, one example as below:
 >mkldnn_verbose,exec,convolution,jit:avx512_common,forward_inference,fsrc:nChw16c fwei:OIhw16i16o fbia:undef fdst:nChw16c,alg:convolution_direct,mb32_g1ic64oc64_ih56oh56kh3sh1dh0ph1_iw56ow56kw3sw1dw0pw1,**1.14307**

avx512_common: the executed instruction set architecture
forward_inference: the convolution type
fsrc/fwei/fbia/fdst: format of input data/weight/bias/destination
algorithm: the convolution algorithm, direct convolution
mb32_g1ic64oc64_ih56oh56kh3sh1dh0ph1_iw56ow56kw3sw1dw0pw1: 
  mb, mini-batch, 32
  g, group, 1
  ic, input channel, 64
  oc, ouput channel, 64,
  ih, input height, 56,
  ....
  1.14307, the runtime of this kernel. 

So, you can see the output are really meaningful.
I think we can simplify the MKL-DNN output from MXNET side and make it more readable rather than the raw data.

- **Text Output**
The text level summary may be not such fancy as the visualization tools. But it's really concise and efficient. The user doesn't need to bother chrome and post-processing json file.

A very good example is from Theano's profiling tools [example](http://deeplearning.net/software/theano/tutorial/profiling.html).

As @TaoLv mentioned, this profile includes the runtime from Class, OPs and their instance (Apply).

> How do you trigger the build?

In one project, we use
```bash
/opt/fst-watchman/watchman \
    --foreground \
    --logfile=/dev/stdout \
    --statefile=/etc/watchman/sierra.state
```
where `sierra.state` looks like
```json
{
    "version": "3.0.0",
    "watched": [
        {
            "path": "/home/vagrant/fastly/Sierra",
            "triggers": [
                {
                    "name": "make-dev",
                    "command": [
                        "/home/vagrant/fastly/Sierra/bin/make_dev_no_args",
                        "/home/vagrant/fastly/Sierra"
                    ],
                    "append_files": true,
                    "stdin": [
                        "name",
                        "exists",
                        "new",
                        "size",
                        "mode"
                    ],
                    "expression": [
                        "anyof",
                        [
                            "match",
                            "*.coffee",
                            "wholename"
                        ],
                        [
                            "match",
                            "*.js",
                            "wholename"
                        ],
                        [
                            "match",
                            "*.scss",
                            "wholename"
                        ],
                        [
                            "match",
                            "*.html",
                            "wholename"
                        ]
                    ]
                }
            ]
        }
    ]
}
```
In another project, we use [ember-cli](http://www.ember-cli.com/). As far as I can tell, that uses the [sane](https://www.npmjs.com/package/sane) NPM package, which in turn uses [fb-watchman](https://www.npmjs.com/package/fb-watchman).

MAC computation would require either dereferencing, or some sort of special case. In the end, we're hashing bytes for the MAC, so as an example we could just have anchors hashed as "reference to X", but then we'd have to protect against the reference being replaced by just "reference to X", so imo we should dereference.

Dereferencing while keeping the anchor in the output document would probably require some serious modification to go-yaml, which I don't expect would ever be merged. And at this point, maybe we should consider operating at the YAML token level instead of at the data level.

Hi,
This is the correct behaviour.
A monitor controls a set of workers (actors) and``bar_foobar`` is the name of the monitor of the ``Bar`` application. If you send a message to ``bar_foobar`` the monitor receives the message.

What the monitor does with the message is up to you. It can send it to one of its workers for example, a random one (workers of a monitor should be interchangeable), or you could pass the worker id in the message so that the monitor pass the message to the worker you are interested in.
But why would you want to do this? What about if that worker died? In any case you need the worker id to do such a thing. So you need a method to get the workers ids first.

Well. What about hourly cronning `luigi --assistant --take-lock --lock-size 4 --workers 10`? It feels very robust to me as there's no need to rely on events. I see three drawbacks.

 1. One is that the new code could take up to an additional hour to start after deploy (you can combine asynchronous and synchronous launches to remedy this).
 2. The second problem seems to what you mentioned before, if you were to have jobs that can take up to 4-5 hours to complete. You could theoretically (and maybe also practically) end up with having 5 assistants each running 1 never-ending job each. 
 3. Normally I would expect about 0-20 jobs to be running, but there's very well possible that there's 50 jobs running at the same time too, if jobs live really long.


Whatever we do, I really don't like the old behaviour, as it seems like "everything" changes when you supply `--take-lock`, if you still like the old behaviour you can get it back with `--take-lock --lock-size 10000`, but with this patch we're kinda hinting that a +1 in lock-size is a sane default when using `--take-lock`.

@Tarrasch, thanks for your PR! By analyzing the history of the files in this pull request, we identified @daveFNbuck, @jcrobak and @interskh to be potential reviewers.

1. I'm more than happy to hear a design proposal of how to implement the Parameter - commandlline relation

 * We currently put state in all Parameter objects. That is just asking for subtle bugs.
 * My current implementation tries to narrow state to only one singleton. You also get the (imo) benefit of that you can verify that the singleton is alive before instantiating tasks.
 * Is there any implementation options I have overlooked?

2. Can you think of any use case where you want to instantiate Tasks before `luigi.run` or `luigi.build`? If not, then I think we shouldn't allow it as it is probably a user bug then.

3. The plan is to extend command line parsing after the overhaul. In particular we at Spotify have seen the need for luigi to semantically understand task parameters. The thing I'm wondering about is why we don't just accept the pagination token as any type and perform no parsing at the Botocore level. The pagination model define the input_token and output_token. For example, from DynamoDB: https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/data/dynamodb/2012-08-10/paginators-1.json#L10-L11. When a pagination token comes back from a service, our response parsers parse it into the appropriate Python data types because we JSON decode the response and walk it with the model. At that point, we have a valid value to pass directly into the input_token of a paginator without needing any form of AST or regex parsing because it's already been parsed out of the JSON response.

It seems like being able to accept the pagination token as a string value that is parsed into a Python type is a requirement of the CLI because it's passed in as a string argument. I'm wondering why we need to have any special handling of pagination tokens at the Botocore level.

I've done some digging here, and FISS is telling you absolutely everything it knows about the error state.  You can verify this by inspecting 
```
/tcga/Tools/python_builds/python__2.7.12/lib/python2.7/httplib.py
```
(e.g. on cga3 server) and observing that the exception class is just a stub
```
class ResponseNotReady(ImproperConnectionState):
    pass
```

with no message, etc.  So the exception name is all that FISS can report when it catches and indicates the exception on the CLI.

In other words, the error is *bubbling up* as much as it can be bubbled.  And we have a different, larger question to consider: is FISS employing the appropriate strategy for credentialing?  If so, then when we delete the application default credentials file then this is the best error we're going to get.

will then send you back to a blank auth.mopidy.com page, do view source and
pull out the client_id/secret and then just use that with no additional
changes.

tokens. Which is what you tried.

This happens because explicitly passing a directory to which the user does not have permission as the cwd to POpen causes a PermissionError to be thrown.  Compare:

```
$ mkdir temp
$ chmod go-rwx temp
$ pwd
/home/me/temp
$ sudo -u another_user python3 'import subprocess; subprocess.call(["pwd"], cwd=None)'
/home/me/temp
$ sudo -u another_user python3 'import subprocess; subprocess.call(["pwd"], cwd=".")'
Traceback (most recent call last):

    with Popen(*popenargs, **kwargs) as p:
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/subprocess.py", line 947, in __init__
    restore_signals, start_new_session)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/subprocess.py", line 1551, in _execute_child
    raise child_exception_type(errno_num, err_msg)
PermissionError: [Errno 13] Permission denied
```

Yes, pypika doesn't enforce any restrictions on which functions can be defined or used (but obviously the functions won't work for database vendors that don't support them).  It includes a lot of functions which are part of the ANSI standard, but not exhaustively.

Take a look at https://github.com/kayak/pypika/blob/master/pypika/functions.py for examples of how to implement functions and https://github.com/kayak/pypika/blob/master/pypika/analytics.py for examples of analytic functions.

Examples of creating a Dialect wrappers can be found in my open PR https://github.com/kayak/pypika/pull/68/files#diff-3e0f8605653b7b954801ec8ac584e7d4 - If you'd like to contribute one for Clickhouse, please open another PR. Right now the dialects are quite basic but potentially any behavior can be overridden.

I can't promise that everything doable in Clickhouse is immediately supported by pypika, but if you run into any barriers, please feel free to create tickets to support them.

Regarding restrictions such as the limit to the number of joins: pypika doesn't enforce any of these kind of rules because the database itself will enforce them.

Hi, this has been proposed multiple times (the most informational thread is #1050, I believe), and the resolution is to refrain from doing it, since Pipenv wants to focus on the application aspect of Python development. In an application context, and it is wildly considered best practice to support only one Python minor version in a Python application.

Note that the `python_version` key is optional. You can always remove it if it causes problems, and document the version requirements otherwise (i.e. in the README). The checks also should generate only warnings that can be ignored if you really know what you’re doing.

I spent a little time messing around and wanted to add some notes...

I see now that when you `pipenv install library` in a new directory it ALWAYS creates Pipfile and Pipfile.lock, So, if you then `pipenv --rm' and `pipenv install new_library` it will then always install the new_library and everything in the Pipfile. 

Coming from the pip & virtualenv world that wasn't obvious to me. Based on a deeper understanding, I _sorta_ get why if there was no Pipfile and pipenv finds a requirements.txt it wants to treat it like a Pipfile and process it. 

Based on an earlier comment, I thought maybe I could workaround by using a blank file. I created a blank Pipfile with a requirements.txt in the same folder and the libraries in requirements.txt where installed when I pipenv installed a new library.

I go back to my original use case and I think I'm at the same opinion:
- I have a project with a requirements.txt what was created using pip freeze and I want to move to pipenv. 
- I want to manually install my libraries with pipenv so I can end up with a proper Pipfile that only has my requirements and not my requirements dependencies. Because when the requirements.txt is processed you end up with Pipfile with all the dependencies added. 

Originally, I moved the requirements.txt up a folder and we see that didn't work. As-is I guess you have to rename requirements.txt or delete it.

Instead, I think pipenv should never automatically process a requirements.txt and instead an --import-requirements (or something) should be added to be used in the migration process. 

However, maybe there is another use case I'm not considering, like trying to co-exist both Pipfiles and Requirements.txt?

@Moritz90 @techalchemy @uranusjr @tsiq-oliverc 

Here is my summary from previous discussion.

# Particular problems and proposed solutions

## Many execution contexts - who shall maintain `Pipfile.lock` file(s)?
Each supported OS and python interpreter contribute to matrix of possible execution contexts.

E.g. Flask supports (at least CI stuff visible in the repository):
- OS Windows (python 2.7 and Python 3.6)
- Linux (python 2.7, 3.4, 3.5, 3.6, nightly, pypi)
- OSX (py - not sure if there are more versions)

It makes 9 different execution contexts which may differ.

Each execution context may have different `Pipfile.lock`.

Who shall maintain them?

Options are:
- Let developers do that manually (NO WAY)
- Maintain only one `Pipfile.lock` for main development platform (which platform enjoys to be ignored?)
- Automate creation via CI (YES)

Proposal: Let CI generate the file by `pipenv install -e .`. Do not include it in repo, help developers to pick proper `Pipfile.lock` as result of automated builds. 

## Developers need predictable environment
When fixing an issue, which may be caused by changes of dependencies on pypi, developer may need simple mean to reproduce the environment from the failing test.

Proposal: 
- for many packages, the pypi dependencies changes are so rare, that they are not real problem
- to fix the environment on it's own, developer may generate the `Pipfile.lock` by `pipenv install -e .` followed by `pipenv lock`.
- to replicate environment from failing test, developer pick the `Pipfile.lock` from the failing test.
- todo: show examples, how to apply `Pipfile.lock` in `tox.ini`.

## CI must reveal broken `setup.py`
Library `setup.py` may be broken (missing dependency in `install_requires`, missing version specifier etc.) and CI test must not hide such problem (by preinstalling omitted dependencies on it's own).

Proposal:
- trust `pipenv install -e .` to provide the same result as plain installation (there are currently some issues with that).
- run plain installation test (without `pipenv`) and possibly compare that resulting `pip freeze` output is subset of what is installed by `pipenv`.

## Updated pypi dependencies may break things, CI shall detect such chases
Some dependency update may break library using it. CI shall detect failure on such problem.

Proposal:
- at least one test must run against unpinned version
- if CI always generates new `Pipfile.lock`, this is non-issue

# CI modes for different library types
In all proposed modes I tried to avoid keeping `pipenv` files in the repository saving developers from maintaining this really complex stuff (automation!!!).

In contrast to my original text the 2nd and 3rd mode do use `pipenv` in CI scripts.

## Mode: Run, Forrest, Run!
Simple package with smaller number of dependencies which are not changing often.

Simply run as before the `pipenv` era and keep things simple to us.

Rare cases when dependencies will make troubles are easy to fix and do not justify making CI more complex.

## Mode: Generate and seal
Each time the CI test is run, generate new `Pipfile.lock` which describes completely the environment used at the moment.

If things go wrong, developer can pick `Pipfile.lock`, apply it locally and do the testing and fixing.

## Mode: Ice Age
When changing dependencies are real problem, CI shall create `Pipfile.lock` once upon a time and keep it using for certain period (a month?).

This makes CI setup more difficult, as there must be at least two different jobs (one generating `Pipfile.lock`, the other one applying it and using in tests).

Warning: `Pipfile.lock` must be update also at the moment, `setup.py` changes dependencies.

Note, that the Ice Age requires Scrat the squirrel type of test which ignores the frozen status and checks against unpinned versions.

# Closing remarks

As seen, the determinism and complexity grows mode by mode.

My proposal would be:
- start simple ("Run, Forrest, Run"). You gain efficiency and speed.
- if things get too complex because of changing dependencies, move on to "Generate and seal". You gain repeatibility in local environment.
- if things are really bad, go "Ice Age" mode. You gain (temporary) determinism.

All gains cost something.

> This seems like a good idea to me because we eliminate some classes and the test class hierarchy maps a little better to the exist APIRequestor hierarchy. Thoughts?

Yes, this is much better! I updated the PR to get rid of the abstract classes. Now we have:
- [`OAuthRequestor`](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/pull/287/files#diff-06e8e6a0633cc19f1fef8fbc334012cfR363) derives from `ApiRequestor`
- [`StripeOAuthTestCase`](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/pull/287/files#diff-fc89950d409dc41fcbbbb30f9009d957R233) derives from `StripeApiTestCase` (and overrides [`REQUESTOR_CLS_NAME`](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/pull/287/files#diff-fc89950d409dc41fcbbbb30f9009d957R234))
- [`StripeOAuthRequestorTestCase`](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/pull/287/files#diff-fc89950d409dc41fcbbbb30f9009d957R210) derives from `StripeRequestorTestCase` (and overrides [`REQUESTOR_CLS`](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/pull/287/files#diff-fc89950d409dc41fcbbbb30f9009d957R211))

[`StripeRequestorTestCase`](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/pull/287/files#diff-fc89950d409dc41fcbbbb30f9009d957R189) is a new parent class for [`APIRequestorRequestTests`](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/pull/287/files#diff-fc89950d409dc41fcbbbb30f9009d957R189) that did not exist previously, that takes care of setting up the requestor and mock client. Previously, `APIRequestorRequestTests` did that itself, but I couldn't make [`OAuthRequestorRequestTests`](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/pull/287/files#diff-f44daad033c958c345472ed9e1c63c2fR384) derive from `APIRequestorRequestTests` and simply override `REQUESTOR_CLS` as it would also inherit all the `test_*` methods.

> The NumPy community has moved away from inheriting from NumPy arrays
> because it raises a whole bunch of problems. An example: suppose someone
> comes up with a fancy new "Gabor++" method that works by creating a Gabor
> kernel, modifying in some weird way, then applying it. Any call to
> np.copy(kernel) will strip away the class and return a bare array. Then
> you've lost your way of applying the kernel.
>
> The other issue is the "surprise" factor: this is a pattern that does not
> exist anywhere in skimage, or NumPy/SciPy for that matter. So now users
> have to wrap their heads around a whole new API for Gabor kernels. Or they
> run g = filters.gabor_kernel(freq) and then type type(g) and get a
> strange class that they've never seen.

Here is a [gist](https://gist.github.com/eprigorodov/b0311434f1381ab08b163682f3d9ba10) that works in the stock ZCatalog and fails under the cached implementation due to inconsistent cache read (tested on commit 9d8aea653c6a729085e41bf48f0fe69cd6ca8f74).

The race scenario is:

* Thread-1 and Thread-2 execute the same method which indexes a new object and then makes a query that should include it
* both threads start at the same time, so they read the same committed version of FieldIndex:
    * `_counter` in both threads equals to N
    * `_p_serial` in both threads equals to 'M'
* Thread-1 indexes its own `object1`
    * FieldIndex increases its `_counter` to N+1
* Thread-2 indexes its own `object2`
    * FieldIndex increases its `_counter` to N+1
* Thread-2 makes a query that should return a result set, containing a newly added `object2`
    * caching descriptor builds a cache key, based on a query, `_counter == N+1` and `_p_serial == 'M'`
    * ZCatalog makes a new result set
    * the query result is cached and becomes visible to Thread-1
* Thread-2 verifies query result and commits its changes
* Thread-1 does not see any committed changes in its transaction, because ZODB isolates transactions on snapshot level
* Thread-1 makes the same query that should returns a result set, containing a newly added `object1`
    * caching descriptor builds a cache key, based on a query, `_counter == N+1` and `_p_serial == 'M'`
    * that cache key is already present in the cache
    * cache returns result set from query made by Thread-2
* Thread-2 iterates result set and reaches an entry corresponding to `object2` document
    * `object2` document is not visible in Thread-1 transaction yet
    * ZCatalog tries to instantiate a result entry and fails with the `KeyError`

The gist contains a conflict retry mechanism similar to ZPublisher. That was mostly done to handle `ConflictError` exceptions that are inevitable in this scenario.

FWIW: I thought it'd be better to be explicit about this. Someone needs to look at each place where None values are indexed into the catalog and decide whether or not that was wanted or even required behavior. Some of them might need to be switched to using a different dummy value.

Another approach would be to ignore any inserts of None but raise a warning instead of an exception.

> bool is a built-in type in C++; there's no enum "implementation" that I'm aware of.

I poked around the C++ spec a bit, and you're right.  Based on what I read in the C++11 spec, as well as some posts I found around the internet, it seems like the way to be portable between C and C++ here is to use stdbool.h.  Unlike C, C++ also doesn't specify (at least as far as I can tell) what the actual values for true and false are, so technically we really need to use `true` and `false`, though I would be very surprised if there is an implementation where they aren't 1 and 0 respectively.

So, I think the safest solution would be

```c
#if !defined(BROTLI_BOOL)
#  if (defined(__STDC_VERSION__) && __STDC_VERSION__ >= 199901L)) || \
      (defined(__GNUC__) && (__GNUC__ >= 3)) || \
      (defined(_MSC_VER) && (_MSC_VER >= 1800)) || \
      (defined(__cplusplus))
#    if !defined(__cplusplus)
#      include <stdbool.h>
#    endif
#    define BROTLI_BOOL bool
#    define BROTLI_TRUE true
#    define BROTLI_FALSE false
#  else
#    define BROTLI_BOOL unsigned char
#    define BROTLI_TRUE 1
#    define BROTLI_FALSE 0
#  endif
#endif
```

> If you're seriously considering this, I just have to know: what is so hate-worthy in the int-as-boolean idiom? :smile:

There isn't anything inherently wrong with it (other than the wasted space, but who cares about that?).  The real problem is that it's not clear what it is; every time I see an int return value I have to look up the documentation to see what the possible values are.  It's not just humans, either; static analyzers and optimizing compilers have no way of knowing what the possible return values are.  Enums would solve this, but they conflict with the standard booleans from C99+.

However, all that is moot now.  Unless I'm missing something, 1.0 has been released, which means the API and ABI are stable.  Changing `BROTLI_BOOL` to an integer now would be a break.  Brotli doesn't specify a C standard for compilation, so C99 or C11 are used (depending on the version of the compiler used), which means existing compiles *of brotli* should all use stdbool.  Basically, what @jchampio was seeing when compiling in C89 mode would happen to *everyone* since the ABI would suddenly be returning `sizeof(int)` bytes instead of `sizeof(bool)`.

I've been thinking more about how to properly delay computation for atmospheric PSFs and also making an intuitive API for this.  Currently, the way to draw multiple PSFs corresponding to the same atmosphere over the same time interval (but at different field angles) is something like this:

```python
atm = function_to_make_atmosphere()
thetas = function_returning_field_angles_of_interest()
psfs = atm.makePSF(..., theta=thetas)  # returns list of PSFs

for psf, gal in zip(psfs, gals):
    obj = galsim.Convolve(psf, gal)
    obj.drawImage(...)
```

Note that this is different than:
```python
atm = function_to_make_atmosphere()
thetas = function_returning_field_angles_of_interest()
psfs = []
for theta in thetas:
    psfs.append(atm.makePSF(..., theta=theta)

for psf, gal in zip(psfs, gals):
    obj = galsim.Convolve(psf, gal)
    obj.drawImage(...)
```

since the latter increments the clock each time `makePSF` is called.  I think it may be more intuitive, however, to have these two code snippets perform the same computation.  To make the latter efficient, I also think we need to delay the actual computation of the PSF to drawImage (currently all the Fourier optics math occurs at initialization).  For photon-shooting, I think this has more-or-less already been accomplished by pulling `SBProfile.shoot()` into the python layer and overriding `PhaseScreenPSF.shoot()`.  I guess something similar could be accomplished for FFT-mode by pulling `SBProfile.drawK()` into the python layer and adding a custom `PhaseScreenPSF.drawK()`?

For boiling atmospheres, where the outer loop ought to be time and the inner loop the object, I think we should mark, in `makePSF`, which PSFs should be computed in FFT-mode.  The first `drawImage(method='fft')` would then trigger Fourier-optics computation for all PSFs currently attached to the `atm` object and marked `fft=True`.  Marked PSFs could still be drawn using photon-shooting, I think, and maybe unmarked PSFs could even still be drawn in FFT-mode, they just wouldn't trigger the time-saving outer-loop = time, inner-loop = PSF computation.

We'd need to think carefully about how to track the atmospheric clock for all of this.  Currently the clock advances for each call to `makePSF`.  Two mutually exclusive potential improvements may be

- Having the user explicitly manage the clock with `atm.advance()`, `atm.rewind()`, `atm.reset()`.  Note that `rewind` wouldn't be available for a boiling atmosphere.
- Passing a `t_start` argument to `makePSF` indicating the starting clock time for each PSF.

Patroni already gives you all control over recovery.conf, it is possible to configure what it will put inside of it after running custom bootstrap script:

```yaml
bootstrap:
    method: <custom_bootstrap_method_name>
    <custom_bootstrap_method_name>:
        command: <path_to_custom_bootstrap_script> [param1 [, ...]]
        recovery_conf:
            recovery_target_action: promote
            recovery_target_timeline: latest
            restore_command: <method_specific_restore_command>
```

Basically you can build any custom recovery.conf just by adding more options into `bootstrap.<custom_bootstrap_method_name>.recovery_conf`

This is already in prod as `12.1.4.dev2`.

## Assessment

I feel there are two distinct problems:

1. Normalising standard metadata. These should be thoroughly documented, and can open the way to Core handling such as type validation, presence check (`reference`…), or behaviour change (how to handle mismatching definition periods…).
2. Exposing arbitrary metadata without any filter. This could be offered as well, but opens room to divergence and typos (`unti` would be exposed as `metadata.unti` rather than raising an error).

## Opinion

I am in favour of supporting both.

- Arbitrary metadata exposure allows innovation to come from any country package, and can be normalised after the fact and moved to Core with top-level exposure.
- Ensuring fully-specified and normalised metadata can be accessed allows cross-packages interoperability.

In the current case, that means to me proceeding with exposing `unit` at top-level **with solid consensus on the format**.

## Immediate action suggestion

Since @benjello's need seems to be urgent, it might be beneficial to prioritise delivery of the arbitrary, grouped `metadata` pass-through. We could learn from actual usage the difficulties involved, and move slowly towards normalisation.

## Long-term vision

I see how the Core metadata addition process could evolve to having convergent implementation of arbitrary metadata in two or three independent country packages.

For example, I see how `reference` is surprisingly not mature yet: while the need is obvious everywhere, the exact format of that property is not.
Aotearoa New Zealand has an [interesting taxonomy](https://github.com/ServiceInnovationLab/openfisca-aotearoa/wiki/Reference-types) for them, and France has mixed URLs and identifiers.
I feel the best would be to expose `reference` under `metadata` until a piece of work is seriously done on defining and unifying `reference` management, including providing tools to assess how properly referenced a country package is.

First of all, thanks @fpagnoux for this thorough research and clear suggestion. We are getting closer to RFCs, and that makes me proud of our team 😊

Now, on to the proposition.

- - - -

## Critique of solution 2

Needlessly complex, as others have already stated.

## Critique of solution 1

I'm not in favour of this `type` property that changes how you're supposed to handle `values`. If you want clients to make disjunctions, then just use a different property 🙂 We could thus have either a `values` property or a `scale` property, and the type is inferred through the presence of one or the other.

Think about the client code:

(solution 1)
```js
if (response.type == 'marginalScale')
    value = response.values[date][threshold];
else
    value = response.values[date];
```

vs

(solution 3)
```js
if (response.scale)
    value = response.scale[date][threshold];
else
    value = response.values[date];
```

Using different properties scales as much, but doesn't induce repetition, makes introspection easier since navigation algorithms will fail early (`"values" is not a property` instead of `Number is not an Object`), and decreases human cognitive load (the root property name defines how to interpret the data structure).

I'm still worried that the interpretation of the thresholds is not obvious in the representation proposed in solution 1: is is the lower or the upper bound? Is it inclusive or exclusive?

Solution: guarantee that there is always a lower bound. In the `impot_revenu` example, that would mean that the `0: 0` pair is present. The responsibility is thus moved towards the legislation, but looking at [`param.xml`](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/5f9a3cf837e3fb68421ae6609f38d489804fff9e/openfisca_france/param/param.xml) it looks like this is already the case.

Thus:

## Proposition 3

```json
{
    "description": "Tranches de l'impôt sur le revenu",
    "id": "impot_revenu.bareme",
    "scale": {
        "2015-01-01": {
            "0": 0,
            "9700": 0.14,
            "26791": 0.3,
            "71826": 0.41
        }, "2014-01-01": {
            "0": 0,
            "9690": 0.14,
            "26764": 0.3,
            "71754": 0.41
        }, "2013-01-01": {
            "0": 0,
            "6011": 0.055,
            "11991": 0.14,
            "26631": 0.3,
            "71397": 0.41
        }
    }
}
```

## Additional questions

Are there any scales that are _not_ defining only a map where keys are [infimi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Infimum_and_supremum), i.e. whose thresholds are not **ascending** and **inclusive**? I didn't see any in looking at `param.xml`, but if that can happen (example: thresholds triggered by deficit, where thresholds would thus be [supremi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Infimum_and_supremum)), then the interpretation is more ambiguous and could mean needing to introduce either a set of different properties such as `ascendingScale`, `descendingScale`, or some metadata.

If this could only theoretically happen, but is not known and seems unlikely, then we can proceed with `scale` and introduce `descendingScale` when the need arises.

It's possible to use varsized int, it's not violation of proto, but it's
better and advised way is to use 5 byte int.
On Jan 6, 2015 12:07 PM, "akopytov" <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> The protocol specification at http://tarantool.org/doc/box-protocol.html
> mandates a fixed 5-byte MP_UINT value for the BODY + HEADER SIZE field in a
> message header. That's how responses are encoded by the Tarantool server
> and requests are encoded by other drivers (I checked tarantool-php).
>
> tarantool-python, however, encodes this field as a variable-length
> MP_UINT, so it can take 1 to 5 bytes, depending on the request size. The
> server handles it correctly, it does not enforce the fixed 5-byte field
> length and accepts an arbitrary MP_UINT. tarantool-python expects a fixed
> 5-byte field in responses (from Connection._read_response):
>
>         # Read packet length
>         length = msgpack.unpackb(self._recv(5))
>         # Read the packet
>         return self._recv(length)
>
> Even though it works when talking to the server (because it always replies
> with a fixed 5-bytes size values), it's still a violation of the protocol
> spec. For example, if a proxy wants to reply to a PING request by echoing
> PING packet data in response (which would be correct assuming clients
> encode PING requests correctly), tarantool-python does not accept such a
> response. In other words, it sends garbage out, but expects valid replies
> back in.
>
> —
> Reply to this email directly or view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/tarantool/tarantool-python/issues/51>.
>

> Do you have more than 2^32 returned data

No, I dont

I did some testing. I believe the error occurs on concurrent use of the same Connection. If two processes try to `self._recv(5)` simultaneously upon recieving two responses from Tarantool, things will go south.

Especially, when I change the version in build.sbt manually and rerun make, only Scala-dependent tasks are triggered and swig-related files are automatically replaced. 
If I change the version through cmake variables and rerun make, some C++ codes are also re-compiled and I have to delete swig-related files generated under the different version to avoid conflicts.
Scala versions are not likely to be modified so often and maybe this overhead doesn't matter so much, though.

The problematic aspect of using Python 2.6 (and any version earlier than 2.7.9, really) is the secure networking support in the standard library - as PEP 466 spells out, the default settings are positively archaic. Using pycrypto instead actually alleviates a lot of those problems, but it's still hard to ensure that dependencies aren't relying on the outdated default behaviours in the standard library modules.

For the time being, the DeprecationWarning approach sounds like a good idea to me - PEP 493 spells out some of the current issues with rolling out the PEP 466 and PEP 476 network security improvements across all widely available versions of Python 2.7, but as those get addressed, then continuing to use Python 2.6 for any activity involving the public internet will be a genuinely bad idea.

To avoid it, there are two choices:

```
env_variables:

2. Not use gevent
