Actually, I ran into another edge case, this happens when an unbounded or inexact requirement in a resolvable is passed and --cache-ttl is used. In this case, if the cached version fits within the bounds of the resolvable requirement, and is less than ttl, only the cached version is returned. But if another resolvable has a requirement that doesn't match that cached version, then it fails.

For example:

```
$ pex --cache-ttl=86400 'thrift' 'thrift<0.9.1' -o test.pex
$ pex --cache-ttl=86400 'thrift' 'thrift==0.9.1 -o test.pex
Could not satisfy all requirements for thrift:
    thrift, thrift==0.9.1
```

In this case, the first pex run pulls down thrift==0.9.0 into the cache. On the second run, it finds the unbounded dependency and pulls the thrift==0.9.0 from cache. It then finds the exact dependency for 0.9.1, grabs that from pypi, but since the first resolvable only pulls from cache, the intersection results in an empty set and Unsatisfiable is raised.

This patch addresses that case by returning both the cached version found and the pypi results. In fact, this patch should mean we don't need to deal with package identity at all.

```
diff --git i/pex/resolver.py w/pex/resolver.py
index e7669c4..bc512ed 100644
--- i/pex/resolver.py
+++ w/pex/resolver.py
@@ -241,8 +241,11 @@ class CachingResolver(Resolver):

       if self.__cache_ttl:
         packages = self.filter_packages_by_ttl(packages, self.__cache_ttl)
-        if packages:
-          return packages
+        # Return both cached packages and the pypi packages. The edge case is if a inexact
+        # resolvable finds a cached version, it needs to return that cached package AND all pypi
+        # packages because the cached version might not match another resolvable which has a tighter
+        # bound or an exact version.
+        return packages + super(CachingResolver, self).package_iterator(resolvable, existing=existing)

     return super(CachingResolver, self).package_iterator(resolvable, existing=existing)
```

This doesn't solve the case where --cache-ttl is not used, but I think as far as pants goes, it passes a cache-ttl by default so this shouldn't affect it.

> Hi! I'm working on a translation app and I would like to take a docx 
> file and create a new one with the same styles and runs, but having 
> the text in a different language. Is there any way I could do this?
>
> I read that changing paragraph's text, removes all styles and runs 
> from it! so, this is not an option for me, since I need to keep them 
> for my new document.
>
> The other approach I tried was using runs object to create my new 
> file. The problem with this is that I have to translate the text of 
> each run separate and the final text doesn't make any sense. Again, 
> not an option for me, because it's a translation app and I need my 
> final text to make sense hahaha
>
> Any other ideas? thanks in advance!
>

That's the whole point of runs.  They tie content and formatting together.

Think of each run within a paragraph as an object containing text and 
formatting.  During translation, the order of these runs within the 
paragraph might change so that the text makes sense, but the FORMATTING 
would change in exactly the same order, wouldn't it? Whatever words were 
highlighted, underlined, (or whatever), would still want to be 
highlighted in the same way in the new run position within the 
paragraph.  So during the translation process, you're not re-ordering 
just text, but text-and-formatting, which is to say, runs.

You might need to break some of your runs into multiple parts because of 
the subtleties of different word orders in different languages, but you 
can figure that out and carry the formatting along with the words into 
the multiple new parts.

So "read" all the runs in a paragraph, re-arrange them during 
translation, and build the new paragraph in the new document out of the 
re-arranged runs.

Good luck.

Hi. I tried to define a table style myself recently and met the same problem as yours. I found a solution and hope it is still helpful to you.
```python
def modifyBorder(table):
    tbl = table._tbl # get xml element in table
    for cell in tbl.iter_tcs():
        tcPr = cell.tcPr # get tcPr element, in which we can define style of borders
        tcBorders = OxmlElement('w:tcBorders')
        top = OxmlElement('w:top')
        top.set(qn('w:val'), 'nil')
        
        left = OxmlElement('w:left')
        left.set(qn('w:val'), 'nil')
        
        bottom = OxmlElement('w:bottom')
        bottom.set(qn('w:val'), 'nil')
        bottom.set(qn('w:sz'), '4')
        bottom.set(qn('w:space'), '0')
        bottom.set(qn('w:color'), 'auto')

        right = OxmlElement('w:right')
        right.set(qn('w:val'), 'nil')

        tcBorders.append(top)
        tcBorders.append(left)
        tcBorders.append(bottom)
        tcBorders.append(right)
        tcPr.append(tcBorders)
```
After this modification, the table should be what you need. Actually I was inspired by [https://github.com/python-openxml/python-docx/issues/105](url). Hope this will help.
You want to use the docx.enum.text.WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH enumeration here rather than docx.enum.table.WD_TABLE_ALIGNMENT. It's the paragraph you're aligning, not the table cell.

Change line 654 from:

```python
return self.xpath('./ancestor::w:tbl')[0]
```

to:

```python
return self.xpath('./ancestor::w:tbl[position()=1]')[0]
```

and line 678 from:

```python
return self.xpath('./ancestor::w:tr')[0]
```

to:

```python
return self.xpath('./ancestor::w:tr[position()=1]')[0]
```

Various `.sqlite3` files that might be present in `/.kolibri/content/databases/` folder are content channels databases that you downloaded at some point. The presence of the database file here does not necessarily mean that the **full channel content** is present also, as in your case.

Yes, once you start re-importing that channel, it will briefly check the previously downloaded resources, and continue from where it was interrupted before. 9GB that you report would correspond roughly to 15% of the channel content.

The main argument against supporting version locking of layers is that it encourages charm authors to lock the versions of their base layers and then forget about them and never update them.  This is especially concerning because, other than informally on the mailing list, there's no notification mechanism for layer updates.

I think this proposal (tracking git sha's or bzr revs in `build-manifest.json` and having the ability to rebuild that) avoids the "set and forget" issue reasonably, since it becomes a build-time locking rather than it being coded into the charm.  This is essentially the "freeze" tool option, but built in to `charm-build`.  Does that seem reasonable?

We would also need to managing locking for the Python dependencies; maybe we can look in to building the wheelhouse using [pipfile](https://github.com/pypa/pipfile) which has built-in support for locking.  I'll open another issue for that.

I was struggling to figure out why we are overwriting `target_device_name` and `target_device_addr` in `on_gap_evt_adv_report()`. According to 13809768, we are doing this in case the name changes between connections. Let's say the user runs:

```
nrfutil dfu ble -ic NRF52 -pkg dfu_package.zip -p /dev/ttyACM0 -n "FooDevice"
```

Then, in the middle of the DFU, the target changes its name to "BarDevice". The user did not provide a target address, and now the name has changed, so the target can no longer be identified based on the information provided by the user. But because we got the `target_device_addr` from the advertisement report and returned it back to `DfuTransportBle`, we will still be able to identify the target device.

My conclusion then is that removing `self.target_device_name = dev_name` should be fine, as we still have the `target_device_addr`.

@hozn just for background: the logging used in Autobahn and Crossbar.io is "structured logging" - this "new" logging system originally comes from Twisted, and because Autobahn supports Twisted and asyncio, supporting/adapting code is in txaio.

The structured logging has a couple of advantages over "classic" logging (that is, logging performatted log messages), and interpolating away structured information before the logger sees it would void the whole point.

So I guess what @meejah says is the actual solution: 

> .. the aio flavour of txaio does need to hook into the default logging properly so it works with your use-case.

Note: not all code within Autobahn and Crossbar.io has been adjusted to use the new structured logging - but that is a bug also;)

OK, got this bad boy merged. Some notes:

1. Part of what took me a while on this one was that it had some small fixes mixed in with big ones. If you can split these into separate PRs in the future, that makes things much easier to review, since I don't have to worry about this thing while doing that thing.

1. I did some surgery on the Minnesota scraper. The main two things I did were to clean up the case name so it didn't have so much junk (docket numbers, court, etc.), and split it off so that there's a minnctapp scraper and a minn scraper. This is needed so that upstream we can categorize the relevant cases into the courts. Otherwise, all the minnctapp stuff would get dumped in with the minn stuff -- not good! 

1. For colo, I added a method to strip down the HTML of the opinion that we're getting. Since the link doesn't point to a PDF, we need to make sure we only get back the HTML we want from the next page. 

1. For coloctapp, something was still broken and I didn't have a chance to figure it out.

All in all, great stuff, thanks for all this.

@mcouthon well, that's actually a pip thing. Even when the package is named `target_mock`, the name when you run `pip freeze` will show `target-mock`.

What we can do, is one of two things:

1. Validate both `_` and `-`.
2. Make sure the package name is always with dashes instead of underscores. I think this is a dangerous option (albeit more aesthetic), so I wouldn't go for it.

I've created https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/5306 for you.

This is because conda-forge has cycles in their dependency graph.

```
DEBUG conda.common.toposort:_safe_toposort(80): Cyclic dependencies exist among these items: 'beakerx' -> 'mkl_fft' -> 'mkl_random' -> 'pandas' -> 'numpy'
DEBUG conda.common.toposort:_safe_toposort(80): Cyclic dependencies exist among these items: 'mkl_fft' -> 'mkl_random' -> 'pandas' -> 'numpy'
DEBUG conda.common.toposort:_safe_toposort(80): Cyclic dependencies exist among these items: 'mkl_random' -> 'pandas' -> 'numpy'
```

If instead you put `defaults` ahead of `conda-forge` in your channel priority list, there's no cycle.

    conda create -n beakerx beakerx -c defaults -c conda-forge

I'm interested in this as well. Most tools respect `NO_PROXY` whereas `conda` doesn't.

@asmeurer doesn't conda use `requests` whenever possible? Asking because `requests` does handle `NO_PROXY` properly -- so you wouldn't have to do anything if you just use their standard API without major hacks?
@natefoo Have we made any progress on this issue with conda 4.3.17?

Ok, I have an update.  The fix will be https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/4626.  I'm going to have to leave the default order of pkgs_dirs inverted.  That's because of assumptions that are hard-coded in conda-build tests that can't be changed without a new conda-build release.  (Definite pain point that conda and conda-build aren't versioned and shipped together.)

However the fix *should* correctly look at the package cache to determine if it's user-writable or not.  To make this determination, we look specifically at the `urls.txt` file within the package cache, since it always exists for any versions of conda in at least the last year.  If the user can open that file for writing, then we infer that the package cache is writable.

I think this at least makes progress for us.  I'll have a path release into the conda-canary channel either today or tomorrow for you to test.

So i've tweaked the debug logging in conda and have found this to be due to `specs` at [line 407 in plan.py](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/887646a59913695019dcd2f204e57fad90ea4f9c/conda/plan.py#L407) having the value:

    ['pandas', 'requests', 'openssl', 'cryptography', 'pip ==']

This was due to us setting pip as a default package using the following command:

    conda config --force --add create_default_packages pip==#{node['python']['pip_version']}

Unfortunately `#{node['python']['pip_version']}` evaluates to nil in our chef recipe so the condarc ends up looking like this:
  
    create_default_packages:
    - pip==

There should probably be some error checking when using `conda config` command.

From the Conda ML, possibly from @natefoo, and related to the request for a `--softlink` option to force soft/symbolic links:

I'm using Conda in CVMFS and am presented with the problem of rapidly growing space usage in the CVMFS repository (filesystem). This is in part because CVMFS does not support cross-directory hardlinks and so installing packages into environments results in multiple copies of the same package contents.

CVMFS is an HTTP-based FUSE-implemented read-only filesystem designed for software distribution which stores the "master" copy of a repository in content-addressable storage (CAS) (i.e. hashed file chunks on a local filesystem) on a host called a stratum 0. Changes are propagated out from stratum 0 servers and ultimately to clients via HTTP, which mount it via FUSE. When you make changes to the repository on the stratum 0 (i.e. beginning a transaction), CVMFS first read-only mounts the CAS using the CVMFS FUSE client. It then uses AUFS to mount a writable filesystem that is unioned with the read-only mount. You make your changes and when done, publish the changes. The difference between the read-write set and the read-only set becomes a snapshot.

This AUFS filesystem supports cross-directory hardlinks just fine. But when the changes are published, cross-directory hardlinks are broken and become multiple copies of the same file.

Conda falls back to symlinks if hardlinks are impossible, but unfortunately, this doesn't work in the CVMFS case, since conda is able to create hardlinks at runtime and doesn't know they will be broken later.

So, is it possible to force conda to use symlinks? The only config option I can see related to soft/hardlinks is to disable the symlink fallback. I need it to prefer symlinks to hardlinks, not the other way around.

For me personally, the main reason to set a default value is to guarantee that a given config option will always be of a certain data type.  By even allowing ``None``, you add an extra data type you have to account for.

I'm not 100% opposed to allowing ``None`` in cases like this, but at the same time I'm not sure I even consider this a bug.  If anything, the bug might be that it should warn you that ``None`` simply isn't a valid default value!  Though like I said, I'm not 100% convinced on that.  Does anyone else have any opinion on whether ``None`` is a good/bad thing to allow in cases like this?

```
In [43]: b
Out[43]: [3]

Out[44]: [1, 2, [3], 3]
```

I.e. the semantic difference between ``append`` and ``extend``.

Say for example, `az ad sp create-for-rbac --role contributor  --name my-app1`
As per command usage, the argument `--name` can be either the display name or an app-id uri and it will generate one if missing. This means they both get set as,
`display-name=my-app1`
`app-id-uri=http://my-app1`

A re-run of the same command will fail because a similar app-id uri already exists.

But, the portal always auto generates a unique app-id-uri thus, I can create a new SP with name my-app1.

This difference of behaviour between `create-for-rbac` az command and portal may be the root cause of the symptom described by @aku105 above.

> I'd be perfectly comfortable straying into "what is more free" territory. A ShareAlike provision places an extra requirement on users of the data, just like Attribution does. Internal names don't much matter of course but my preference is to clearly highlight that the SA data in OpenAddress is special and has extra obligations on the part of users.

I agree with @NelsonMinar, though I admittedly I have strong feelings about this debate (still, I find his formulation of the dynamic hard to argue with).

Speaking more specifically to the alternative proposal: "relicenseable" is more positive but also much less useful, I think. For one, "can I relicense this data?" is probably not at the top of most users' lists of concerns. For another, it is probably not an accurate distinction in this case: in my experience reading these things, non-CC attribution licenses in the collection generally forbid relicensing under terms other than those stated by the source. I suspect that only our PD and CC-BY sources are safely relicenseable.

I don't think it's worthwhile to poke at this hornet's nest in any detail (I honestly have no idea where the derivative work line would lie for this kind of information, to say nothing of how it might apply in international contexts). Better not to address the relicensing question at all. We can speak (softly) to the question of _use_. I think telling people what new rights they can assert over the data is much iffier, and best left to them to sort out with their lawyers.

> Also, maybe we could have a note up there giving some indication of how much data is in each download.

As far as I know there is no good recipe for choosing the number of states, unless you have some expectations about the True Number from the domain knowledge. 

Using likelihood wouldn't work because a model with more states always (over)fits the data better. You could look into asymptotic criteria ([AIC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Akaike_information_criterion)/[BIC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bayesian_information_criterion)), but if the dataset is big enough, the effect of the penalty they impose would be negligible.

```diff

-                for factory in app._middlewares:

             resp = yield from handler(request)
 
@@ -446,6 +446,16 @@ def run_app(app, *, host=None, port=None, path=None, sock=None,
                 # add_signal_handler is not implemented on Windows
                 pass
 
+        # I know I'm doing that for only one app, which won't fly with subapps.
+        # This is just a hack to show my thinking.
+        for resource in app.router.resources():
+            # another dirty hack; implementing this whole thing properly will probably take some
+            # thinking about interfaces of a few classes.
+            http_handler = resource._routes[0]._handler
+            for factory in app._middlewares:
+                http_handler = loop.run_until_complete(factory(app, http_handler))
+            resource._routes[0]._handler = http_handler
+
         try:
             print("======== Running on {} ========\n"
                   "(Press CTRL+C to quit)".format(', '.join(uris)))

```

Available [here](https://github.com/butla/aiohttp/commit/3ab39dd9752d7e130d9aae87f22eae0cacbdb992).

For my application (and middleware factory), the app can respond with 401 about twice as fast: 2488 requests/second on commit [d2236cfe](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/tree/d2236cfe75ccd518c684a1b3f3a6a2c8da0267a1) (the last one from master) vs 4090 reqs/sec on [3ab39dd97](https://github.com/butla/aiohttp/tree/3ab39dd9752d7e130d9aae87f22eae0cacbdb992) (mine)

It'll matter when the middleware factory does a bit more complex setup, or calls something over the network, e.g. get some public keys to verify JWTs.

Right now, with the current implementation of middlewares, I'll be doing that this one-time preparation outside of the framework, so I'll have a factory, that produces a factory, that produces the wrapped handler. Smells like corporate Java :)

I know that changing the way it works right now can potentially break someones code, so we totally shouldn't do it. If we'd be going with my approach, we can introduce a new parameter - `middleware_factories` that would be mutually exclusive with `middlewares`. As for the approach with streamlined middleware coroutines, I don't know what be a good name for the parameter. But I also think that setting it should turn off the old middlewares.

Doing a good job on the UI/UX hiding is a lot of work. I think there are
two possible routes with the classic notebook:

1. Not implementing it in the UI/UX but making sure the metadata is
preserved it it is there.
2. Implementing it in the exactly same was as JupyterLab.

(I am deliberately ruling out a third option, which is to re-design the
UI/UX it from scratch in a completely different way from JupyterLab).

I am fine with 2 if there is someone willing to do the work and follow the
ongoing JupyterLab UI/UX for it (which is still evolving). However, as a
whole, I think our time is better spent not implementing significant new
UI/UX features on the classic notebook at this point.

However, there may be another reason to hold off on doing this in the
classic notebook. When we implemented it in JupyterLab, we had to create
additional levels of nesting and a more complicated DOM structure to manage
the collapser (the thing you click) and the placeholder (the thing that
shows when input/output is hidden). The same things would have to be done
in the classic notebook implementation.

Because of this change in the DOM structure, the performance with large
notebooks became a huge roadblock for us. The reason is that Firefox has a
bug that causes the rendering of nested flex box's to scale as O(N^2). That
was causing even medium sized notebooks to take ~45 seconds to render.
However, even performance in Chrome was painful. It was enough of a problem
that we have delayed the JupyterLab beta release and have been working hard
to address the performance issues. We know how to fix it (move away from
nested flex boxes!) but that is requiring a significant refactor of the
views of the JupyterLab notebook.

Because the current DOM structure of the notebook (pre-refactor) is very
similar to that of the classic notebook, I think the classic notebook
implementation will run into the same exact performance problems and
require a complete restructuring of the DOM, CSS etc. While we have never
really thought of the COM/CSS of the classic notebook as being a "public
API", in reality, many extensions rely on those things and would break.

cc @sccolbert who is tackling the performance issues on the JLab side.

Hi Summer,

There's two ways you could get rid of this warning.

First option would be to add a header to the top of the VCF file, e.g.:

##INFO=<ID=STRAND,Number=1,Type=String,Description="insert description
here">

Second option is to use the warnings module from the Python standard
library, e.g., in Python 3:

thanks for this, it's a good point to add to the documentation: by default, browser is session scoped (long living among various tests) and cookies are cleared on every test, BUT, only for CURRENT domain.
that's a limitation of selenium, when you call a method to delete cookies, it only does that for current domain, as i think it goes up to the browser security limitations as well. see http://stackoverflow.com/questions/16127496/selenium-webdriver-delete-cookies-on-all-domains
So for you, there are basically 2 ways:
- simplest, but slowest, switch to function-scoped browser, either by overriding a fixture `splinter_session_scoped_browser` to return False or just by passing a command line option `--splinter-session-scoped-browser=false`
- a bit more complicated, but smarter and faster: override a browser fixture like this:

```python 
@pytest.fixture
def browser(browser):
    browser.visit('your third party url')
    browser.cookies.delete()
    return browser
```

Thanks for the PR @pierreozoux! I was actually on the fence about this issue for a while. After thinking about it a bit. I don't think we want this.

My reasoning is:
1) As pointed out by @PythonProdigy, running `docker` without `sudo` will not work for some users so we shouldn't been giving them invalid instructions. Taking this a step further, running `docker` with `sudo` should always work so let's prefer the more reliable instructions.
2) I'm not sure if we should be encouraging people to run `docker` without `sudo` as there are serious security implications of doing so (read [this](https://docs.docker.com/engine/articles/security/#docker-daemon-attack-surface) if you're not familiar). With this in mind, if we are encouraging anything, I think we should encourage the more secure method which is to run `docker` with `sudo` instead of adding your user to the `docker` group.

Regardless, thanks again pierreozoux!

@Wuyanan520 That is, evidently, a separate bug, as I am able to reproduce it, you can report as a separate issue.

That said, I think the "correct" behavior would be to parse `201508` as YYDDMM, because the way the parser works if it sees a 6 digit string and it doesn't already have a year/day/month, it will figure that's what it's dealing with, so the logic will go like this (for unspecified `dayfirst`):

1. Break up the string into 20, 15, 08
2. Check to see if it's MMDDYY -> 20 is too big to be a month, so eliminate that choice.
3. Check to see if it's YYMMDD -> 15 is too big to be a month, so eliminate that choice.
4. Check to see if it's YYDDMM -> 15 can be a day and 08 can be a month, so confirmed.

If `201508` is intended to represent August 2015, then, if possible, you should change the format to "2015-08" or "2015.08" or "2015/08" or something like that, or special case these 6-digit dates yourself.

Additionally, I'll note that if you know that all your dates are of the format `YYYYMM` (or just in general you know the exact format of the dates), then you probably shouldn't be bothering with `dateutil.parser` at all. You can just do this: `dt = datetime.strptime('201508', '%Y%m')`. Of course, this will resolve to `2015-08-01`, while dateutil's parser (probably unexpectedly) returns missing values from *today*'s date unless specified. In case that's a behavior you want, you can probably just do `dt.replace(day=datetime.today().day())`.

The question is what is Telethon's goal? The mobile phone application or the web client shows messages immediately. On the other hand, channel messages received via Telethon/API are typically delayed between 0 - 60s. They are also often received in bursts after a delay. This could be too much for bots as well as fluent communication.

Can we get the same functionality in Telethon?
Is it even possible or does Telegram API limit us?

Please see this example:

```
[17:43:18] CRYPTO TROLLBOX (1129575249) (59 sec) SC targets?
[17:43:18] CRYPTO TROLLBOX (1129575249) (41 sec) I need a good binance group with trading signals, anyone??!
[17:43:18] CRYPTO TROLLBOX (1129575249) (28 sec) Add me
[17:43:18] CRYPTO TROLLBOX (1129575249) (25 sec) Short 720, later 1000
[17:43:18] CRYPTO TROLLBOX (1129575249) (14 sec) SHould i purchase etn or not
[17:43:18] CRYPTO TROLLBOX (1129575249) (12 sec) ?
[17:43:18] CRYPTO TROLLBOX (1129575249) (04 sec) Qtum smart to buy? Because of the big announcement

```
(Lonami: I utilized the minimalistic code we used today while fixing the previous problem to collect these messages.)

There is time the message was received via Telethon on the very left. It is 17:43:18 for all messages. Then follows channel.title, channel.id and message age computed as the difference between current time and message.date expressed in seconds.

I usually witness the phenomena that the messages always come in at the same second. Lets say new message can come at 12:10:18, 12:11:18, or 12:12:18 and so on. (With very slow forward drift, it will change to 19 in some time.)

We are, in fact, getting updated once per minute only so the messages could be aged anywhere between 0 an 60s.

@BibMartin I've made a coupe of updates to the pull request:

* The `popup_attribute` is now a `popup_function` parameter - if you pass in a function it should take a GeoJson feature and return a string value that is stored in a new `_popupContent` parameter in the feature properties. This is calculated when you intialise the GeoJson class. If you pass a string it acts as before, and simply uses that attribute as the popup value.
* I've put the `{% if this.popup_attribute %}` in the template as requested, so the user can update the `popup_attribute` at a later stage. Since it calculates the results of the function on `__init__` though setting a function here won't work.
* I've added tests for the geojson to test_folium.py
* I've added the popup_function parameter to the `choropleth` method on the map object as well so it will pass straight through

I also took a look at adding the Popup object, but I'll have to have a bit more of a play with it. First though I'd like to update the TopoJson methods so it's consistent with the GeoJson and also has a `popup_function` available.

Since schema validators are designed to validate a single object instance unless `pass_many` is explicitly `false`, I think the intuitive behavior is for `pass_original` to return the corresponding original object instance unless `pass_many` is explicitly `false`.

 - Picking the original preprocessed object out of a list is inconvenient at best and impossible at worst.
 - Validation of a nested list is more appropriate on a parent schema instead of an overload of the child schema validator.

@taion Yes, the `validate_bob` example is contrived: you would use a field validator in that case. I just wanted to show that this API allows for the `validate_*` methods proposed in https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/153#issuecomment-84448786

For two reasons:

* You're defining a new class with each invocation. I'm fuzzy on the details, but I don't think classes would be referenced out due to their relationship with the module in which they are created. Even if they would be normally GC'd that is made irrelevant by the schema registry. 

* Internally, marshmallow keeps a record of all schemas defined so it can do resolution when you reference a schema by name rather than by class (e.g. you do `'MySchema'` rather than just `MySchema` - note the quotation marks). Schemas are stored as a map of name to list of schema class AND fullpath to schema class. 

Note that because of reference counting in CPython (I'm making an assumption on your environment here), this doesn't result in double memory for a single registry entry.

tl;dr your infinite loop causes infinite registry entries which uses infinite memory. And despite the registry, this might happen anyways due to module-class relationships.

Input to BLSTM must be a sequence of vectors. When you say the input shape is [32,32,512], you mean it is [Batch, Seq, channel], right? 

CoreML will see it as [?,?,512] at conversion time, since Batch and Sequence can be variable and provided at runtime. 

The [documentation](https://apple.github.io/coremltools/coremlspecification/sections/NeuralNetwork.html) here might be useful to understand the special way CoreML handles the batch and sequence dimensions. Essentially these are runtime dimensions that are not stored in the model, but instead are to be provided at runtime.

Here's our V2Mixin (e.g. for endpoints that use JSONAPI):

```
class V2Mixin:
    pagination_class = V2Pagination
    parser_classes = (
        rest_framework_json_api.parsers.JSONParser,
        rest_framework.parsers.FormParser,
        rest_framework.parsers.MultiPartParser,
        BulkActionJSONParser,
    )
    renderer_classes = (
        rest_framework_json_api.renderers.JSONRenderer,
        rest_framework.renderers.BrowsableAPIRenderer,
        JSONApiCSVRenderer,
        JSONApiGeoJSONRenderer,
        JSONApiAggregateRenderer,
    )
    metadata_class = rest_framework_json_api.metadata.JSONAPIMetadata

    def get_queryset(self):
        """ This lets us specify prefetch_for_includes on the viewset.

        This allows us to prefetch based on the includes specified in the URL:
        prefetch_for_includes = {
            'item': ['item__property', 'item__tags']
        }
        """
        qs = super().get_queryset()
        if not hasattr(self, 'prefetch_for_includes'):
            return qs

        includes = self.request.GET.get('include', '').split(',')
        for inc in includes + ['__all__']:
            prefetches = self.prefetch_for_includes.get(inc)
            if prefetches:
                qs = qs.prefetch_related(*prefetches)

        return qs

```

Some of the things we need to do for quantum:

* A custom `__new__(cls, *args)` method where we process args, instantiate
using `Expr.__new__` and then set some slots.
* A custom `doit` method (is that supported?)
* A custom `_eval_rewrite` method
* Other custom `_eval` methods such as `_eval_inverse`, `_eval_power`,
`_eval_adjoint`, `_eval_trace`. Some of these are for base sympy logic to
work, others are custom to quantum.
* All the custom print methods for pretty, latex, etc. that return strings
(not print to stdout)
* Custom `__mul__`.

Speed might be an issue eventually for certain types of algorithms in
quantum computing, but I don't want to start in C++. The speed critical
will be very localized and we could drop into C++ at that point if needed.
I just want to make sure our custom Basic subclasses in Python will have
the right information so that when we do drop into C++, it will all work
(without us having to go back and implement everything in C++).

The bigger place we struggled in building the quantum stuff was in the
actual overall model and APIs of sympy. I am fine if the overall model is
the same as sympy, but that stuff doesn't seem to be documented anywhere.
The most subtle stuff was things like:

* All the magic in `__new__` with @classmethods that process args.
* Attempting to have custom multiplication logic - the combination of
python's model for `__mul__` and sympy made this extremely subtle.
* Assumptions
* No clear APIs and policies for when things are evaluated (in __new__
versus in methods such as doit).
* Documentation about the protocols (such as _eval_*) that objects need to
implement to work with other parts of the code base.

As always, I care much more about those things than speed. But we can't
fully ignore speed either. Thanks!

You're very close to a working solution! The bits around `var_val` seem unnecessarily complicated; you already have the numeral in the variable `v` and you can use that as is, no string conversions are needed, e.g. `a_new = v` should do just fine. Also, instead of the `*_new` variables you could just keep a single `Z3_ast` and then, in each iteration, just `Z3_mk_and(...)` the new inequality. 

In general, when working with the C API, you are responsible for the reference counting of expressions, i.e., every time Z3 returns a Z3_ast (and many of the other Z3_* object types), you have to call `Z3_inc_ref` on it and once your code is done with an expression, you have to call `Z3_dec_ref` on it. This is not optional. Without that, you will get segfaults and incorrect results very often, so fix this first. Or, use one of the higher-level APIs for prototyping (e.g. the C++ API has the `expr` class that takes care of this for you).

I agree with you that these are real issues for companies that do not wish or can not make their work available under a MPL compatible license. This is a difficult to navigate area for all of us who are trying to create software that uses open-source projects in a commercial setting. As a commercial entity with a business model that is incompatible with MPL like licenses it is in my interest to have work licensed under BSD.

(Speaking purely for myself here) To make `nteract` sustainable I think `nteract` needs to work out what is in its long term interest and put those interests first. Anything else makes no sense. These interests might not overlap 100% with those of any other organisation or company. IMO the fastest way to reach a mutually beneficial position is to openly discuss the needs of each party and then make an informed decision.

I would like to see some discussion and then conclusion on what `nteract` the community thinks is its long term goal and hence what is in its interest. Once this is clear it will be much easier for me to decide whether or not making this change to the license is a good move or not.

Gymrat is a framework for creating new lifters for angr. It provides nice syntax for writing the vex that instructions translate into and automates a bunch of the infrastructure necessary for a lifter. For an example of such a lifter, see [here](https://github.com/angr/angr-platforms/blob/master/angr_platforms/msp430/instrs_msp430.py). 

Expanded reality is a branch devoted to removing angr's reliance on a byte being 8 bits (see the cLEMENCy architecture). 

In terms of spotters, I've gotten started on implementing them. It appears that when Vex fails to decode an instruction, it returns the partial block with a jumpkind of Ijk_NoDecode. I currently detect when an instruction has failed to decode using the jumpkind and then try all of the other lifters (in true angr style) until one works, and then concatenate all of the blocks together. I should have that finished off and working by tonight or tomorrow. At that point we will need a lifter for these instructions and some test cases with the svc instruction.

`monotonic` is a very confusing library, I was using it from the `time` module on a very famous drone not too long ago, I quickly found out it wasn't the proper way 💩  💀 .

`time.monotonic()` was introduced in python 3, [and the monotonic lib defaults to that if you have it](https://github.com/atdt/monotonic/blob/master/monotonic.py#L53), if you don't you need to use `monotonic.monotonic()` which gives output compatible to that of `time.time` on python 2.

To clear this up, the monotonic module only exposes a function called `monotonic`, in other words [`monotonic.monotonic()`](https://github.com/atdt/monotonic/blob/master/monotonic.py#L154-L160) is what you need to call, we are dealing with a very very basic 1 function module here.

Just a follow up on this as I'm not clear what the conclusion was?
We similarly have a lot of views that use state from the session for anonymous users, this is a pretty typical usecase. 

To test this with the normal django test client this is done via eg:
```
        session = self.client.session
        session['some_key'] = 'some_value'
        session.save()
        response = self.client.get(reverse('users:signup3'))
```

Where the get request now has access to the session data which the view can use as needed so that the test can pass. 

However attempting a similar thing with django-webtest with `self.app.session` instead of `self.client.session` just gives an `AttributeError: 'dict' object has no attribute 'save'` as @bochecha mentioned,  an instance of Session hasn't yet been made and attached to the app. 

So instead doing, 

```
        self.app.set_cookie('sessionid', 'initial')
        session = self.app.session
        session['some_key'] = 'some_value'
        session.save()
        self.app.set_cookie('sessionid', session.session_key)
```

Works well. (Note 'sessionid' is the default value of settings.SESSION_COOKIE_NAME)
Presumably an appropriate PR for this could be included so that this doesn't need to done for everytest that needs to set the session and the usage is the same as Django.

I've just had a look at the LHCbDirac api and it still looks like the jobs are constructed with the jobID in the name:
```
This method is used to supplant the standard job wrapper output data policy for LHCb.

      The initial convention adopted for user output files is the following:
      /lhcb/user/<initial e.g. f>/<owner e.g. fstagni>/<outputPath>/year_month/<jobID/1000>/<jobID>/fileName

      But, if prependString is set, then the following is adopted:
      /lhcb/user/<initial e.g. f>/<owner e.g. fstagni>/<outputPath>/<jobID>_<prependString>_fileName
```

The latter carries the limitation that the ```fileName``` component can't have a ```_``` character within it.
(This is due to something at the Dirac level beyond our scope)

I plan to catch and throw a Ganga error on this as it would be nicer than waiting for the job to fail due to LHCbDirac not returning a similar error.


I've included pseduo-code which shows how the generic Dirac deals with user LFN construction.
The name mangling is very similar only the behaviour to LHCb.

```
user_prefx = VO specific Operations option used for building user LFNs
...
basePath = '/' + vo + '/' + user_prefix + '/' + <initial e.g. f> + '/' + <owner e.g. fstagni>
...
if not outputPath:
  outputPath = <jobID/1000>/<jobID>
else:
  outputPath = OutputPath
lfn = os.path.join( basePath, outputPath, os.path.basename( fileName ) )
```

Both vanilla Dirac and LHCbDirac construct similar enough LFN that the base of the LFN can be taken to be:
```
/vo/user_prefix/<initial e.g. f>/<owner e.g. fstagni>/
```

Then the fileName is effectively decided on top of this.

The LHCb naming convention for user files appears to be:

```<outputPath>/<jobID>_<prependString>_fileName``` or ```<outputPath>/year_month/<jobID/1000>/<jobID>/fileName```

However the Dirac naming convention is only to use:
``<outputPath>/fileName`` or ``<jobID/1000>/<jobID>/fileName``

I think if we add the ```prependString``` within DiracFile it would be valid as we can change the LFN structure for the Dirac backend to be:

```<prependString>_fileName``` from within Ganga.


There is just the decision on whether we want to limit vanilla Dirac users from not using the ```_``` character in their filenames?
We can't dynamically change the fileName to not include a ```_``` character within Ganga as this is the output from the user. However, I think it's perfectly fine to add it as a 'generic' requirement that if the user wants to use the new attribute corresponding to the ```<prependString>``` then they should be aware of this limitation.

I worked around this issue by installing OpenAI Gym and Mujoco by making an Conda environment.
```
conda create -n test python=3.5.2
sudo activate test
cd ~/.mujoco
git clone https://github.com/openai/mujoco-py
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH="$LD_LIBRARY_PATH:$HOME/.mujoco/mjpro150/bin"
pip install -e ./mujoco-py
cd ~
git clone https://github.com/openai/gym.git
cd gym
pip install -e .
```

The following lines need to be added in .bashrc located in the home folder.
`LD\_LIBRARY\_PATH="\$LD\_LIBRARY\_PATH:\$HOME/.mujoco/mjpro150/bin pip install mujoco-py"`

To use Mujoco-py and OpenAI Gym:
`source activate test`

To finish: `source deactivate`

Indeed, the problem here is that the gi extension outputs its documentation in separate subfolders, one for each language, but when linking to a symbol which is output in one of these subfolders from a page not handled by the gi extension (in our case overview.markdown). I will fix this to link to the first of the languages required from the extension (by default, ['c', 'python', 'javascript']), but I would advise modifying your sitemap to have this page handled by the gi extension, either by using it as the gi-index (with --gi-index, still keeping --gi-smart-index), or as a subpage in the sitemap:

```
index.markdown
        gi-index
                gi-overview.markdown
```

The reason for doing that is that in this case, you'll get one version of that page per language, with the symbol links being translated :)

Yes, `Mac::HandleEnergyScanSampleRssi` is another example of "busy-looping" in a tasklet waiting for an event from the platform drivers.  This implementation is designed to sample the radio's RSSI as quickly as possible and stop when `Mac::HandleMacTimer()` is called.  Of course, the timer handler is only called when the platform alarm fires.  The current OpenThread examples always processes the platform drivers before each call to `otProcessNextTasklet()`.

My earlier comment was addressing the use of `--with-ncp-bus=uart` and `--with-ncp-bus=spi`, which isn't technically what the `--with-*`/`--without-*` arguments are intended to be used for (see my previous comment for details). The current usage is inconsistent with how those types of arguments are intended to be used.

But I'm not going to recommend we hold back for that. We already aren't super consistent to begin with.

Yes I can take this task. What I see there are three things to do:
 - TX path: Use seed-id only when IPv6 address is not an RLOC, else add padding (0x01, 0x00) [RFC2460 4.2]
 - RX path: Whenever seed-id is elided, retrieve seed-id from source RLOC address
 - 6LoWPAN: Add support in Extension Header compression/decompression to elide or retrieve padding if needed
 
The last change is needed to make any sense of this change, else we don't save any bytes by eliding seed-id, because of extra padding.

If the angle of attack is acceptable, please give me recommendation about those two issues, since I'm not yet familiar with different layer communication:

 1) How to pass RLOC16 value to the MPL module? Can I pass NetIf pointer, then cast it to ThreadNetIf and use ThreadNetIf->getMle().getRloc16() inside MPL::InitOption?
 I am asking because ideally I would not like to call MLE::getRloc16() function inside generic IP6.cpp or IP6_mpl.cpp files - to not mess with ISO/OSI layer model.
 
 2) Same situation with isRoutingLocator(), unfortunately I need use also Mle::isRoutingLocator(), since Address::isRoutingLocator() checks only IID.
 It may not work if IPv6 global address, generate e.g. by RFC7217 have RLOC form but with different prefix hence we cannot called it RLOC.
 
Also if you are worry about incompatibility with other stacks, I can use some #define for TX path, however I think implementation of RX path should be mandatory. Obviously I don't want to decrease number of passing certification tests.

But… man I just understood how pyFFTW works and using a Numpy-like interface for the builder functions is a nasty trick ! It's very confusing since what happens inside a `pyfftw.builder` has nothing to do with a Numpy ufunc.

I think your doc should mention from the beginning that the neither the FFTW object nor the builders  trigger an actual FFT, and the FFTW object is more than just a Python storage class, but reserves a low-level memory space where FFT**s** happen and plans the best method to do the conversion **later**. So it just prepares the space and schemes. 

The most confusing part is the need to send twice the same arrays to the builders, once calling the builder method and storing the output in a variable, then calling the variable again with the same arrays. It should be made clear that the first call uses only the shape and types of the arrays to initialize the memory space, and the second actually perform the FFT.

`mysqldump` should default to using UTF8 as the character set for its output ([source](http://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.7/en/mysqldump.html#option_mysqldump_default-character-set)).  If necessary, you can instruct it to use a different character set via the `--default-character-set` option.

Are you running `mysqldump` on Windows?  See this note in the [mysqldump documentation](http://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.7/en/mysqldump.html#option_mysqldump_result-file):

> **Note**
>
> A dump made using PowerShell on Windows with output redirection creates a file that has UTF-16 encoding:
> 
>     shell> mysqldump [options] > dump.sql
> However, UTF-16 is not permitted as a connection character set (see Section 11.1.4, “Connection Character Sets and Collations”), so the dump file will not load correctly. To work around this issue, use the `--result-file` option, which creates the output in ASCII format:
> 
>     shell> mysqldump [options] --result-file=dump.sql

@bmerry - The story is the same for both base class pointers and `std::shared_ptr<Base>`: it doesn't discard type safety as long as the type is polymorphic.  It *does* need a `dynamic_cast` (via `std::dynamic_pointer_cast` for a `shared_ptr`) to do a runtime RTTI check as to whether the pointer actually is the derived type, and the get the derived pointer (which doesn't have to be equal to the base pointer, particularly when multiple inheritance is involved).

Yes, python modules are shared libraries and need `-shared`.  It's also preferred to *not* link to python itself (`-lpython2.7`); the necessary symbols will be available when the module is loaded since it will be loaded via python itself.

Hi @wushichatong ,

Unfortunately, I believe you've encountered a variant of a known bug, which is that the Certbot nginx and Apache parsers frequently fail to parse configuration files correctly when the configuration files contain non-ASCII characters (such as Unicode-encoded Chinese characters, for example). This problem is especially likely when the non-ASCII characters are found in a configuration directly that Certbot needs to understand, but they can even happen in many cases when the non-ASCII characters are merely found in a comment!

We recognize that this is a bug in Certbot, because both Apache and nginx permit non-ASCII text in configuration files and users are certainly entitled to use it. However, our current parsers do get confused in many such situations.

For more information about the status of this bug, I suggest following

https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/5337 (or similar related issues)

https://github.com/certbot/certbot/projects/2

In the meantime, you could work around this problem by

* switching to a different Let's Encrypt client (although I don't know for sure whether there's another client that can modify nginx configurations and that doesn't suffer from a similar bug)
* using `certbot certonly -a webroot` instead of `certbot --nginx` (this is less convenient, because you have to have the web server set up to serve static files from some location on disk, and you'll have to edit the nginx configuration file yourself to install the certificate at the end)
* temporarily removing the non-ASCII text from your nginx configuration files

I'm sorry for the inconvenience that this bug is causing you.

Sorry to chime in after @schoen already gave feedback and you implemented `--reuse-key`, but I think we should offer only `--key-path`. In general we should strive to keep the number of flags to a minimum, and `--key-path` is strictly more powerful than `--reuse-key`. It can meet a wider variety of needs.

The only reason to additionally offer `--reuse-key` is, as Seth said, to make this particular use case easier. However, the only use cases where retaining the same key matters are already advanced options, like using DANE TLSA with certain configurations, or using HPKP. I think it's acceptable to expect users of those advanced options to specify a full path to a key. Especially because the key path will be saved in the renewal config, so the path doesn't need to be specified again on renewal. @schoen, @bmw, what do you think?

Hi @Kramerican ,

Thanks for the bug report.

Currently the Apache process uses `apachectl` or `apache2ctl` `graceful` (detected based on the OS), while the nginx process uses `nginx -c yournginxconf -s reload`. There's no use of `systemctl` on any platform under any circumstances. Can you think of a particular reason why these methods would lead to the problems that you've been seeing?

If not, could you also share some of your associated log files, including the web server error logs associated with the restart, and the Certbot logs associated with the attempt to get the certificate?

Do you know how long your web servers take to reload? I think both of these plugins have a hardcoded sleep that might not be long enough because they don't know how to tell when the reload has completed (at least this is definitely true of the Apache plugin under some circumstances).

I think in your case, Certbot was actually still running on your server, probably waiting for user input. Certbot uses [OS system calls](http://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/009695399/functions/lockf.html) to provide locking which should automatically be released when Certbot exits. This even happens if you cause Certbot to exit by running something like `kill -9`.

I'm glad you found a workaround, but in general the best way to work around this problem is to kill the currently running version of Certbot so a new instance can grab the lock.

I think you mentioned in person that you'd ruled this out, but I can't remember why:

### Option 5: Add a `ProxyPass /.well-known/acme-challenge/` directive pointing to a `standalone` server running on a special port

[According to docs](https://httpd.apache.org/docs/trunk/en/mod/mod_proxy.html#proxypass):

> Ordering ProxyPass Directives
> The configured ProxyPass and ProxyPassMatch rules are checked in the order of configuration. The first rule that matches wins. So usually you should sort conflicting ProxyPass rules starting with the longest URLs first. Otherwise, later rules for longer URLS will be hidden by any earlier rule which uses a leading substring of the URL. Note that there is some relation with worker sharing. In contrast, only one ProxyPass directive can be placed in a Location block, and the most specific location will take precedence.

This could be combined with a no-op RewriteRule for `/.well-known/acme-challenge` at the highest-level part of the config, [using the L flag](https://httpd.apache.org/docs/2.4/rewrite/flags.html#flag_l) to terminate processing by mod_rewrite.

This is documented [here](http://vatlab.github.io/SOS/doc/documentation/External_task.html#Option-walltime-181) but I should perhaps update the example. Basically sos accepts `walltime` in both seconds (integer) and `HH:MM:SS` (string) and convert it to seconds for consistency. Then for each task queue, it should be converted to an queue-specific format, something like

```
walltime=${walltime/3600:02d}:${walltime%3600/60:02d}:${walltime%60:02d}
```
I hesitated

1. if we should provide a function to make it easy to convert to the most widely used format. 
2. if we should use `HH:MM:SS` as default.

The reason for integer `walltime` is for

```
#PBS queue=${'short' if walltime < 3600 else 'medium'}
```
but I suppose users can also do

```
#PBS queue=#{'short' if int(walltime.split(':')[0]) < 6 else 'medium'}
```
if we chose to use `HH:MM:SS` as internal format.

sure, I will notify him if we confirm that these are actually bugs, rather than intended behavior.

In this particular case, the splitting of the cubic curve seems to be done intentionally.

 The `SkOpEdgeBuilder` class (responsible for converting a SkPath into a set of contours and segments) is calling a method named `ComplexBreak`; the inline comment says:

```
// Split complex cubics (such as self-intersecting curves or
// ones with difficult curvature) in two before proceeding.
// This can be required for intersection to succeed.
```
https://github.com/google/skia/blob/87a7372928043db83fd9019eada25cfcfd1b0706/src/pathops/SkOpEdgeBuilder.cpp#L269-L273

This method checks that the curve is monotonic (either increasing or decreasing) in both X and Y, otherwise it splits it to make it so:

https://github.com/google/skia/blob/6fdbf6161bcb299f855fd467ac4cdbf38c14a5cb/src/pathops/SkPathOpsCubic.cpp#L244

So.. the monotonicity of the curves this seems to me like a requirement to make the curve-to-curve intersection algorithm to work properly.

I think we need to check for such "complex" curves upfront, and if we detect that they are still present after we called skia to remove the overlaps, try to re-join them.

There are a couple ways that we could approach this problem, depending on how correct we want to be.

We can partition an arbitrary dependency DAG into a series of nodes that we can know are safe to execute in parallel. A straightforward algorithm for this will produce a set of series of nodes (set of antichains) that is the same size as the length of the longest dependency chain in the graph, which is a big improvement over where we are today.

We can also move towards a full-blown DAG scheduling system, where steps have dependencies and get scheduled by the engine executor as soon as the steps they depend on complete. This design is flexible but also more complex. It has the added benefit of allowing us to schedule replacement deletions during the plan.

The HDEV experiment was indeed scheduled to be replace by another (unrelated) experiment from ESA (European Space Agency) - that experiment has been delayed (multiple times) and HDEV is now safe for the foreseeable future (that other experiment seems to have been cancelled). 

There are indeed times when the video stream (encoded from Johnson Space Center) is actually offline - although thankfully that is very rare these days.  The longest that channel was offline was during Hurricane Harvey when much of JSC was shut down (except for critical systems in Mission Control and with the James Web telescope).  The building where this stream is encoded was not part of those critical systems and there was some water damage in the building.. which resulted in systems being shut down for about 5 days.

@stasm - no problem. I've managed to update to 0.6 spec already, as per the last commit above.

I'm now getting failures on Python 2.7 only. It turns out there has been a change that `Identifier.name` is now a bytestring (`str`) object on Python 2.7 only. Before it was a unicode string, and this is causing the failure (resolver tries to use unicode strings only).

Before:

    >>> FluentParser().parse('foo = { bar }').body[0].value.elements[0].expression.id.name
    u'bar'

After 0.6 changes merged:

    >>> FluentParser().parse('foo = { bar }').body[0].value.elements[0].expression.id.name
    'bar'

 On Python 3, it was and still is a unicode string. I could work around this on Python 2.7, but this seems like an unintentional change, and doesn't match other things e.g. `MessageReference.name` etc.

I found the problem. The object that I have is coming from Postgres database using `psycopg2` library. As you know we are using `ujson` to dump these objects. Here is the result:

```python
>>> import datetime
>>> import ujson
>>> import rapidjson
>>> import psycopg2
>>> def encode_timestamp(obj):
...     if isinstance(obj, datetime.datetime):
...         return int(obj.timestamp())
...     raise TypeError(repr(obj) + " is not JSON serializable")
...
>>> rapidjson.dumps(datetime.datetime(2017, 2, 27, 10, 20, 0, tzinfo=psycopg2.tz.FixedOffsetTimezone(offset=0, name=None)), default=encode_timestamp)
'1488190800'
>>> ujson.dumps(datetime.datetime(2017, 2, 27, 10, 20, 0, tzinfo=psycopg2.tz.FixedOffsetTimezone(offset=0, name=None)))
'1488190800'
>>> rapidjson.dumps(datetime.datetime(2017, 2, 27, 10, 20, 0), default=encode_timestamp)
'1488208800'
>>> ujson.dumps(datetime.datetime(2017, 2, 27, 10, 20, 0))
'1488190800'
>>>
```
 If there is **without** timezone object, `ujson` treats as **UTC** time!

Whoops, I guess I [won my bet](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/aee2676fd5096daf2849db9756242a30e0aa1f30/trio/_core/_ki.py#L81-L83).

I'm not sure if this should be considered a bug in trio or in raven – we can work around it in trio by using a string key, but raven should perhaps be robust against this anyway :-).

You're going to run into some other issues with raven too, I think:

* Make sure to `import trio` before you `import raven`, because it looks like they both mess with `sys.excepthook`. If you `import raven` first, then it'll take over `sys.excepthook`, and then trio will notice that someone has taken it over and skip installing its hook, which means you won't get good tracebacks for `MultiError`s. If you `import trio` first, then raven will notice this and delegate to trio's hook after running its own, so that works better. (Unfortunately trio can't do the same thing, because it wants to *replace* the usual hook, while raven wants to *augment* the usual hook.)

* If raven is trying to capture frame information from unhandled exceptions, then it's not going to return very useful results for `MultiError` exceptions, because it won't know to peek inside and find the inner exception traceback frames.

It looks like raven has some [precedent for shipping integration code for specific libraries](https://docs.sentry.io/clients/python/integrations/), but I haven't looked any closer to see if its extension points are powerful enough to fix these issues...

I guess as a first step I'll file a bug on raven and see what they think.

Either: Just `os.path.join` it with `--path` so that the user can use relative paths (like just a filename) or absolute paths that can be outside of `--path`.

I've been thinking about this for some time now, as the `search_footprints.geojson` implementation was never supposed to be long term - alas I never came around to change it.

What I would expect from this function:

- `search_footprints.geojson` should be saved under `--path` (if given) otherwise at the current directory
- make it a keyword argument, instead of a flag, and use the filename given, otherwise fall back to `search_footprints.geojson`

That way we could get the best of both worlds - convenience and more options for the users to controll it. Added benefit is, that this change would be backwards compatible.

This would mean:

- `sentinelsat --footprints` results in `/<current dir>/search_footprints.geojson`
-  `sentinelsat --footprints s1_germany_footprints.geojson` results in `/<current dir>/s1_germany_footprints.geojson`
- `sentinelsat --footprints` results in `/<current dir>/search_footprints.geojson`
- `sentinelsat --path /some/other/dir --footprints s1_germany_footprints.geojson` results in `/some/other/dir/s1_germany_footprints.geojson`

Thank you @tjingrant for the detail clarification! as @gramalingam said, my personal concern here is this would impact the op spec somehow. so, besides adding this annotation, let's also refine our op spec (via the op description right now) to clarify the exact format that an op could accept in this PR.

The inputs and outputs are defined here: https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/onnx/onnx.proto#L248--L249. The inputs and outputs of an ONNX model are well defined, even if we don't have any node in the graph. ONNX models can have two identical outputs without any problem, because any tensor/var is only defined in initializer or operator. We can still keep SSA property.

I am not trying to remove Identity operator, actually, I also would like to keep it in the spec, if it will greatly ease the exporting work. :-)

@skottmckay the way the spec is written currently, Y_h must always be returned, even if it happens to be redundant with Y. 

Y_c is in a bit of a strange place - I believe there has separately been discussion about adding an additional output to LSTM which is the concatenation of cell states (i.e. <new output> is to Y_c as Y is to Y_h). But it doesn't affect this PR
